Unfortunately, I agree with previous posts regarding this toy. While recommended for children over 3, it has very inappropriate comments for the target age group. The sayings are filled with attitude that is more geared for teenagers than preschoolers and young elementary children. I gave this  truck to my 4 1/2 year old son for Christmas. While he likes any truck that moves and talks, every adult that has been around Wrecky has thought the same thing as me - "hide it until he forgets about it then get rid of it!" In addition to the comments it makes, it it rough with its motions. This makes it a hassle to use indoors and it lives up to its name in this regard- it wrecks everything in its path (including any child's body parts within the room). The box and description do not have much details on what it says, so I was surprised by the comments and noises Wrecky makes (this was a lightning deal, so I didn't have the time to research it more, which in retrospect I wish I had been able to). Matchbox simply missed the mark with comments meant for older kids in a toy marketed to younger children.

When we first opened this game, we were all excited.  It looked like it promised to be SO much fun.  The game comes with a large blue arena and three discs that fit inside with different ruts patterned in them for a different performance of the game.  There are two zip type strips that slip through the two tops that come with the game.  There is a handle in the zip type strips.  The idea is that you hold the starter tool after slipping the zip strip into the top and then quickly pull the strip out of the top and place it in the arena.  The tops spin all over the arena knocking into each other and the last top left standing wins.  This game would be more fun if:  1.  The arena was bigger.  More room is needed for the tops to really spin around nicely  2.  The inner discs were more varied in design and sturdier.  3.  The tops were equal in performance.  In this kit one of the tops is a dud. Plain and simple.  My family is not overly thrilled with this game- in fact we were bored.  Who wants to play a game when one top is always slanted against and loses?  This game has potential, but for our family, it was a big FAIL.

The LEGO Movie 2 trailer that I saw didn’t include Benny, but I know that kids will enjoy re-enacting his movie scenes with this BENNY’S SPACE SQUAD BUILDING KIT (70841; 68 Pieces). The spacesuits are printed with the planet symbols (no stickers to frustrate small children).  There are two vehicles (space shuttle, lander car), and some tools/weapons.  As always, the pictorial assembly  instructions are simple, clear, and easy to follow.  The kit, which is suitable for children ages 5 and up, has standard LEGO elements (not Duplo).  There are many small pieces.

This ANTELOPE MOON JIGSAW PUZZLE (1000 Pieces, Round, No Dust; for Adults) is a highly enjoyable jigsaw puzzle for expert assemblers.  The black and white picture, and the confusing moon crater lines, makes the assembly “by picture” very difficult.  However, the puzzle goes together in satisfying fashion–albeit slowly–if you assemble it “by the shapes of the pieces”.  The round puzzle shape also helps with assembly, because the interior pieces are curved differently on the inner and outer edges (inner being closer to the puzzle center).  This is a puzzle that should only be gifted to a jigsaw enthusiast who welcomes challenger puzzles.

The charming Unikitty cartoon series made me a Unikitty fan, because it always makes me laugh. I like this UNIKITTY UNIKINGDOM CREATIVE BRICK BOX BUILDING KIT (433 Piece) very much.  It provides the elements for building many of the series characters (Unikitty, Puppycorn, Richard, Master Frown, Square Bear, Feebee, etc.), plus a Unikitty castle, and lots of scenery.  Hawkodile and Dr. Fox are NOT included, but they’re available in other sets (see [[ASIN:B078W9N8LV Party Time]],  [[ASIN:B07BMH5L8B Dr. Fox’s Laboratory]]).  The set is packed in a big, white LEGO storage box; the box is NOT completely full of bricks, but this means there is room to store other Unikitty sets if you purchase them.  The bricks for the figures’ heads are printed with their faces--small children won’t need to apply stickers.  The set has three building instruction booklets with pieces in corresponding numbered bags.  (The presorted, numbered bags of pieces are standard with larger LEGO sets.)  The many small building projects are great for very young children.  The building techniques are all basic, simple ones.  The elements in the set are all standard LEGO elements (they’re NOT Duplo bricks), so they’re compatible with other LEGO sets you may already own.

This BGRAAMIENS 1000-PIECE JIGSAW PUZZLE (“Blooming Color”; Challenge Blue Board Round Jigsaw Puzzle) is extremely difficult if you approach it in the conventional way--by (1) laying out the pieces face up, (2) assembling the border first, and (3) filling in the puzzle by referring to the picture on the box.  The irregularly shaped pieces, the use of colors in small areas across the entire puzzle, and the confusing curved lines in the design add greatly to the difficulty.  Fortunately, this puzzle has letters printed on the back that make the puzzle easier to assemble.  If you sort the pieces by A, B, C, etc., you will have a much more manageable puzzle.  It’s still quite difficult, but the assembly will be entertaining and enjoyable, rather than a Herculean labor.  (See my photos of the 54 pieces that are marked with “A”, to see how difficult the puzzle really is.)  The puzzle pieces are not as nice as the ones in Ravensburger puzzles, but they're made of fairly heavy cardboard, and they fit together precisely.  When you find the correct piece, you know that it's the right one.

I have been around Barbie dolls my entire life.  My mom collected them and my sister got into it.  My daughter has had a few here and there, but none of them was the quality that this doll is.  I did not expect it to be fantastic quality, but it even fell short of my lower expectations.  WHAT IS THE DOLL LIKE? The doll comes in a clear plastic package which is not overly attractive, in fact it looks stark and not like the usual Barbie stuff.  The doll itself is packaged with two pieces of plastic sticking out from inside her head.  It is then attached to the board in the back so you have to cut those pieces to get it off of the backing.  That means you have to be careful of the hair.  Once you managed to do this, you have to stick the plastic ends back into the skull of the doll to keep the sharp ends from pricking your child or you.  The outfit is kind of cute and the colors are attractive.  The doll itself is a rigid plastic doll- not the bendable twist and turn type that they have had in the past.  The head piece has a knob type attachment which makes it able to move in almost any direction.  The problem is in this case, the head is so easy to move it is borderline bobble head.  HOW DOES THE HAIR COLOR WORK? The hair color looked like it would be a lot of fun!  It has a plastic piece that is similar to a flat iron in shape.  Inside the *flat iron* piece are a group of colored waxy blocks.  The idea is that you comb the doll's hair and separate it into strands.  You press the flat iron type piece over the hair, squeeze it shut as you draw the hair through and it leaves the color on the hair.  The hair is waxy in feel, probably to make the color work better than traditional Barbie hair.  In this case, however, it does not seem to do much for the process.  The hair colors are not too great. They come off as crumbly and waxy and very spotty on the hair.  If you run your fingers over it, you will get some on your fingers.  This will stain so if your child plays with this near anything white or light colored, it could stain it.  The color blocks are almost like you mix waxy crayons with silicone to make it a bit softer.  The result is uneven kind of unimpressive color on the hair.  It is not overly bright and does not look like the pictures promised.  THE BOTTOM LINE My daughter was not impressed by this doll.  The body is very rigid, making for a boring play toy.  The hair is very uneven and has a waxy feel to it.  The colors come out crumbly and uneven, and color blocks probably will only last for a day or two of constant play and then run out.  The colors will stain some things so if you plan to let your child run free with this, you may regret it if you have anything white you don't want stained.  The head is so easily moved that it is unpleasant to play with and use.  The outfit is cute and colorful and the shoes are not too bad either.  My daughter kept saying that she expected more substantial color that was like a watercolor marker or something instead of a crumbly waxy substance that barely sticks to the hair in a few random spots.  She kept taking out her other Barbie dolls and saying how much more she liked those.  I give this two stars because it has a nice outfit, and if you want a disposable toy for your child, it will serve the purpose.  If you want a sturdy toy they will play with over and over, I am thinking this might not be it for you.

Overall- okay if you’re very gentle with the figures and there were no errors when built.  The ones I received were not put together correctly. Male’s neck was over top of the chest. Female’s shoulders are really messed up and do not move correctly. Wish I would have seen this prior to tearing open the boxes, can’t really return them now):  PROS: the concept. Range of motion for the stand- stand can be posed in multiple ways, helpful for awkward positions. Small sizing is great!  CONS: unfortunately a lot... some joints are very loose and will not hold without support. Some joints are too tight! I don’t mind the tight joints cause they hold but takes a while to get them to move and then you feel like you’re breaking it, but you’re not. Knees for the female are not as flexible like the males. My male neck is overtop of the chest, so I can’t move the head. Shoulders on female are awkward and not correct- shoulders are not moveable. Loose parts feel like they fall apart any second. Not as moveable as the picture shown or the instructions show. Just poorly made and not worth the money.  I understand these figures are detailed in joint motion. They are also small. However, some toys are just as movable and have less problems.  Value for money - very poorly made, cost is too high for product rating. Can tell they are mass produced and no quality check. Would return and consider another brand if I didn’t tear open the boxes in excitement. Excitement plummeted after trying to pose them in simply positions.

JIMU robots are somewhat challenging to build, but I like them because they're robust when completed and easy to drive (using the free iOS or Android app).  This UBTECH JIMU ROBOT ASTROBOT SERIES: COSMOS KIT (387 Parts and Connectors) has the parts for three different builds:  a cute dancing robot (Astron), a tractor-tread robot (Astrobot), and a tractor-wheeled robot (Rover).  All three models have eyes that light up in colors.  If you want, you can build one model, take it apart, and build another.  However, be warned that the components are extremely hard to take apart.  The components are compatible with the components in other JIMU robot kits--if you want to create your own robots, you can combine different kits.  (The JIMU components are NOT compatible with LEGO Technic elements.)  JIMU robots are controlled with Bluetooth--pairing is easy, once they're turned on or plugged into a power source.  They don't have separate controllers, so you need a tablet or smartphone to work them.  It’s a good idea to charge the controller cubes before you start building--charging takes about an hour--so that you can play with the robot as soon as you're finished building it.  Building instructions are NOT included in the box, except for a Quickstart guide that tells you where to get the detailed instructions.  You have to download the JIMU app to get the building instructions.  The detailed instructions are presented in 3D--at each step you can rotate the model 360 degrees and look at it from every angle until you’re sure that you’ve got it right.  Older kids should be able to complete these robots, and to program robot moves beyond the basic built-in moves by using the Blockly coding system.  Blockly is part of the downloaded JIMU app.

This comes with bugs ect. pictures on the slides, 3 pictures on each Plastic  slide. There is a little slide drawer with all the pictures on them. That Keeps this neat and organized . Tell the kids, Do NOT put there little fingers on the slides or they will smudge them. Hold the plastic slides on the side edge.  No more having young children to focus there one eyes. The Kiddos can see out of both eyes. Focusing magnifier is 5X. You need 3 AAA batteries,  press ON, and Bindi Irwin ( her father was the Crocodile hunter) will tell you All about the bugs, animals Your child is looking at. There is a fact button, answer button, even a Quiz button, and sound can be adjusted.  All the kiddos Love this learning toy. This also makes a very good Birthday day present, Xmas, or just getting the young kids a very good educational learning toy. I recommend this Talking Microscope .

To be honest, I purchased two of these types of cats... One is this one.. the "FurReal" brand and the other was a "Joy" brand... the cats looked nearly identical, however, the other brand - $25.00 cheaper was significantly better.  This one sounds horribly mechanical, jerky - almost looks like the cat is having a seizure.  One eye is bright and shiny, the other is dull and scratched.... In order to get the cat out of the box I had to literally destroy the box, otherwise, it would totally be going back - in a second.  I'm disappointed to say the least.  Even the fur was nicer on the Joy brand! The FurReal brand also did not "go to sleep" either... it was constantly moving - (I see a LOT of batteries being used). The meow sound is very odd and fake. Honestly, if you really think you need to purchase a robo pet - try the Joy brand.  It was more real and smooth in movement... the meow, still fake, was sweeter... you could actually feel the purr... and it seemed to be more interactive.  It's not perfect either, but a better buy and more satisfying experience. I always update my reviews if new info would be useful -however, not being able to return this was an expensive donation item - so unlikely there will be much more to report.  :(

So it's just a worse value.

I didn't expect anything great for the price and I knew this set would probably be small but I expected something a little better and bigger.   The wood tracks would be difficult for a three-year-old and my two-year-old grandson wouldn't be able to do it. Its not that the system is hard for a youngster to figure out, it's just that the tracks don't fit quite right and the train doesn't stay in the track grooves all that well, especially around corners. It's a rough cut and a youngster will likely derail here. Update Here: "My grandson has managed to put the tracks together except the bridge. Still, the pieces don't fit smoothly."  My next gripe is the plastic bridge that comes with it as the second straight piece. It is a sloped hill and when the engine and coal car go over it tends to derail over the hump. Additional cars make it do so more.  The cars are small and the track is about 18 "inches from end to end. The bodies are wood, but the wheels and lower frame are plastic. The detail is just OK. I bought the Percy engine and another engine with a back-hoe shovel for about $10 each bringing my total to about $42. I feel I didn't get much for the money.  If I had it to do again, I would probably just buy the vehicles and skip the set with track and let my grandson play with the cars on the floor. I bought the wood set probably out of nostalgia and because I thought it would be good quality. Quality is not that bad but not nearly as good as the old days. Next time I go to a fair and see someone selling home-made toys for kids, I'll buy one.  I thought I might add more pieces to this set and buy more tracks if the set was good, but will not now.

I ordered this Lego train set, not sure who would want to build it. I have 2 kids in their early 20's - one in college, the other one who just started his first full time job. Then there's my husband and me, both in our mid 50's. It sat around for over a week without being touched. Then one Saturday night, my husband asked me if I would like to build it with him. I've never been much of a Lego builder. Both kids were and my husband was when he was a kid. I think my brother used to have some and I would build houses with them. Well, it took 2 nights to complete it, but we successfully built this train set. Wow! This is quite the Lego set! The instructions were easy to follow, as we expected with Lego. There were some extra pieces if needed. The train power source required a total of 10 AAA batteries.  My husband, who is a tech guy and loves all things nerdy/geeky really enjoyed playing with this train. It works really well. You can control and adjust the speed. We did learn that if you go at maximum speed, it jumps the track.  There are working headlights. It's really a cool set! We had a lot of fun with it. We're still having fun with it because it's still set up on the table.

This LEGO STAR WARS EPISODE VIII RESISTANCE BOMBER BUILDING KIT (75188; 780 Pieces) is fun to build; and when you’re finished, you have great “Star Wars” toy to play with.  The set has 780 pieces, and the finished model is 13” high x 15” long x 11” wide, which makes it a medium-sized model.  The model’s working features include a bomb release, some spring-loaded shooters, rear gun turrets that open for a minifig, elevating rear guns, a 2-minifig front cockpit, and a removable top section (for easy access). There are several included character minifigs.  I love LEGO building sets (the larger and more complicated the better), and I love the LEGO “Star Wars” sets for their accurate depiction of the battleships and machines seen in the movies.  This model really captures the look of the Resistance Bombers from Episode VIII (“The Last Jedi”).

A large square cardboard box with plastic shell encase this small rectangular plastic game.  When we opened the box, we thought items were missing because the package was so much larger than the game and there were no visible instructions visible anywhere.  On the rear of the box is a picture of a family of four, a man, woman, girl and boy, all with their mouths agape as they appear to be thrilled with an opportunity to slap the large TURBO SLAM! button.  My family does not look like the people on the box and we never opened our mouths like the people on the box.  We love to play Scrabble but this game did nothing for us.  Fingernails were broken while attempting to slide out the drawer which contained the playing cards and instructions, but we had no difficulty inserting our own 3 AAA batteries with our own screwdriver.  The Turbo aspect of this game which includes "music, sound effects and other cool features" did not "boost the action into high gear!" as the game advertises.  It's simply too cheaply made, loud and annoying, much more than the original SCRABBLE SLAM.  The box has written, "More Ways to Play!  Go to [...]."  I could not find anything at the website which even recognized this game and therefore saw nothing about more ways to play.  We adults thought all sounds of this product were annoying.  I doubt that young children would be entertained for long by this game and would not consider giving it as a gift or including it in a toy drive for children.  I cannot recommend this game.

I’m a lover of the really big LEGO sets, especially the Creator buildings (e.g., Cinderella’s Castle; the Detective’s Office) and the Technic heavy construction equipment models (e.g., the Bucket Wheel Excavator).  In my opinion, this medium-sized set, the DISNEY FROZEN ELSA’S MAGICAL ICE PALACE (41148) is one of LEGO’s very best efforts.  The set has lots of beautiful transparent blue bricks, and a couple of specialized large panels for the castle that are transparent blue with embedded silver sparkles.  The transparent blue bricks exactly match Elsa’s blue from the “Frozen” movie.  If you’ve seen the movie, you will love the spiral staircase that expands and rises up with a twist of the ball at the top--very much like the icy castle walls that rose up magically in response to Elsa’s sweeping movements in the movie.  As is always true with LEGO sets, it's fun to see, as you build, how the simple bricks become furniture, chandeliers, doorways, and other details.  The set has minifigures of Olaf, Elsa, Anna, a snow monster, and small snowmen.  The balconied castle is furnished with a chandelier, a bed with coverlet, a table and chairs, a music stand, and a kitchen.  Stickers printed on sparkly silver paper go onto the moving castle doors to complete the dazzling look of the ice palace.  And despite all the detail, this set is fairly easy to build.

This was pretty disappointing. You cannot do anything without buying ingredients separately. If you’re set on getting it, I’d recommend at least buying sodium alginate and calcium lactate. I believe everything else can be purchased at a regular grocery store.  My 9 year old found a few “experiments” she was interested in, and she does like the tools. Honestly though, just buy a few test tubes/candy molds/a whisk/pipette and find experiments online. The muffin tins are a pain, just use a regular muffin pan.  I wouldn’t buy this again or give it as a gift unless you also buy the ingredients needed for multiple experiments. Bare minimum, I’d include: sodium alginate, calcium lactate, dry yeast, yogurt starter, and several fruit juices/cordials. Otherwise you’re giving a gift that will inevitably lead to a disappointed child and frustrated parent.

First, I'm going to keep it and use it. Even though I am a Prime member, I just hate sending things back. So here goes....  The pictures do not show a true visual comparison of the size of the strap, using the controller as reference. The strap is VERY large, with a wide, padded area for neck comfort. It is actually big enough to use as a shoulder strap for small bags. It is too big to store anywhere in my Mini carrying case. I did the logical thing, drilled a couple of holes in the Mini hard sided carrying case and am using the strap for that. Then, when I need it for the controller, It's right there.  The clip rings that are included are just simple aluminum rings. They will do the job. But, they are crude and simple. They look like something I dug out of my miscellaneous hardware bin, and very out of place on the controller.  One of the screen protectors included has a 'flub' in the glass that prevents it from properly sealing to the screen.  This set up is functional and durable. But, thats the nicest thing I can say. I think I could have made a better choice.

This GLISTELLO 1000-PIECE JIGSAW PUZZLE FOR ADULTS--COLORFUL BOATS, MARSEILLED is a good quality puzzle, highly appropriate for jigsaw puzzle experts.  It has many similar pieces that present a challenge to assemble, because they need to be placed by shape as well as by color.  The puzzle pieces are small, which makes the 1000-piece puzzle fit easily on a worktable.  The fully interlocking pieces are thick enough to be handled easily, and the interlocking tabs don’t bend if the pieces are handled with ordinary care. The pieces don’t click together like a Ravensburger puzzle pieces, but they fit together unmistakably by feel when properly joined.  Unfortunately, the pieces don’t fit tightly enough to allow joined sections to be picked up and moved without falling apart.  Completed sections need to be picked up on a sheet of paper, or pushed along the tabletop, to reach their proper locations in the puzzle.  I wouldn’t recommend this puzzle for group assembly, because of the many pieces that are visually similar in color and shape.  Puzzles like this one require a lot of sorting of pieces in advance by color and shape if the actual assembly is to proceed quickly.

This LEGO TECHNIC 4x4 XTREME OFF ROADER BUILDING KIT ((42099) 958 Pieces) produces a toy that is incredibly easy and fun to drive.  The off road vehicle is controlled entirely by an app (the LEGO Control+ app), which is available for free from either Google Play or the App Store.  It’s probably easiest to install the app on your iOS or Android phone, so that you have an easy-to-hold device that feels like a game controller.  The LEGO Control+ app has two control modes:  one-touch and multi-function.  One-touch shows you the vehicle, and you control the direction (forward, reverse, turning) by moving a finger on the touchscreen.  Multi-function shows you a dashboard with controls at left (turning) and right (forward, reverse).  Multi-function gives you easy access to a series of driving challenges (e.g., hairpin turn, etc.).  The build itself is NOT particularly easy.  The instructions are clear, but there are places where it’s tricky to insert everything correctly.  Once built, the Technic model is rock-solid--it won’t come apart, even when you drive the vehicle over obstacles, or off a 4” high “cliff”.  (As with any Technic model, you should check each step of assembly carefully, because Technic elements don’t come apart easily when you make a mistake.)  There are three different motors in the model.  They are powered by six (6) AAA batteries (not included).  The vehicle is definitely NOT underpowered.  The kit includes a big sheet of stickers, which are reasonably easy to apply.  The vehicle shell is NOT printed at all--all of the orange/yellow patterning and sponsor ads come from stickers.

This NEUSCHWANSTEIN CASTLE XXL puzzle is not for beginners, or for the faint of heart!  Any 2000-piece Ravensburger puzzle is difficult, simply because of the number of pieces, but this puzzle is probably above average in difficulty.  The puzzle measures 52" wide by 24" high--it's a panoramic puzzle--so make sure you have cleared a wide space for it.  The XXL size means that the fine photo details are spread over more than one puzzle piece, and this increases the difficulty.  The puzzle is easiest to assemble if you stand over it as you go, so that you can see the bigger, overall picture.  Ravensburger puzzles always have color areas to help you.  That's true of this puzzle, but the sky--there's lots of it!-- has less color variation than usual.  The border pieces are easy to find and assemble, but when you start on the remaining puzzle, it really dawns on you what a challenging task you have undertaken.  There are piles of dark blue, light blue, autumn-colored, and mostly-white pieces that look very much alike. For me, the hardest area was the lower left side, with all of the blue trees; and the big snow-covered tree at the right was far more difficult than I expected.  The castle was easy, and the autumn-colored trees were easier than you would think from looking at the box photo.  Despite its difficulty, this puzzle was a pleasure to work.  The photo is just so beautiful!  Working at a comfortable pace (one to several hours at a stretch, but not every day), I needed about a month to finish this one.  As usual, I found the Ravensburger craftsmanship to be extraordinary--the pieces fit together so nicely that mistakes were few and far between.  I highly recommend this puzzle for expert (addicted) jigsaw puzzle enthusiasts.

I noticed three things about this robot. 1) It's hands have a prehensile (opposable) thumb that you can move. But, there's no spring action to keep it closed. So, it can't actually HOLD anything. Why make the thumb movable if you can't make it useful? 2) The 'proximity sensor' built into the front of the robot doesn't seem to stop it from running into things. And, the worst, 3) The IR sensor that the robot uses to respond to the controller is in the robot's chest. So, if it's facing away from you, it will NOT respond to the controller commands. The IR sensor should have been placed in the top of the robots head, so it could pick up the signal from any direction.  The robot is cute. It has a lot of flashing LED's for eyes. It 'dances' (actually shuffling a lot). It talks and plays music. And, if you have time and can stay in FRONT of it, you can actually program some basic moves into a routine.  But, because of the three design flaws I mentioned, I really can't see any kids enjoying it that much. More than likely, the kids will get frustrated with it, and throw it across the room. SMASH! No more robot.  Sorry, 1 star.

Like many others I owned the original Radio Shack version of this 130-in-1 electronics kit decades ago.  True, this Elenco version isn't very sturdy.  It's light and flimsy.  But the overall layout, components, and experiments you can wire together are about the same.  The 159-page real paper manual that comes with it is a plus (the PDF is also available for download from Elenco's web site).  Each experiment has an overview, a schematic, and wiring connection-by-numbers instructions.  Many useful and educational analog circuit designs are included (plus one digital gate logic IC).  No microprocessor, computer, smart phone, or software updates required.  Yea!!!  OK, now the quality issues.  The first experiments I correctly wired together didn't work at all.  I installed two different sets of 6AA batteries (Amazon Rechargeable then Duracell Alkaline).  Still nothing.  I returned the first one back to Amazon, got a replacement the next day.  Both had the same problem.  Both had a sticker with Date Code 0119.  Hmmmm, manufactured the same day, same problem.  I did some basic troubleshooting to try to figure out the problem.  It turns out the design or manufacture of the battery holder is defective.  There is a gap of about 1mm between the + side of each battery and terminals in the holder.  Two small plastic tabs at that location prevented the battery connection from being made.  My "fix" was to jam a wadded up piece of copper tape to fill the gap at each of the 6 positive battery terminals to complete the battery circuit (aluminum foil would work fine too). Hopefully Elenco will crack the whip at the Chinese factory to fix this, or they'll see a lot of returns for this very simple and silly defect.  Check recent reviews to see if the problem has been fixed in newer batches from the factory.  If you don't have a voltmeter, you can construct a simple circuit to see if power from the batteries is making it to the front panel.  Install the batteries and try lighting up the decimal point of the digital display by connecting: 25-121,24-119 or 25-124,24-122  Most of the experiments I've tried so far worked.  But the AM radio receiver circuits are iffy.  The only one I could make work was #113: Two-Transistor Radio.  I used the supplied long green wire raised up high as the antenna.  The signal levels seem too low to work for #112:  Crystal Set Radio and #109:  Operational Amplifier AM Radio.  Quality issues aside, I'm keeping it.  I'm having too much fun with it.  And I have great respect for the designers who created the original analog circuits that can be recreated using this kit.

I bought this toy thinking it would be good to use on my doorway jumperoo.  The jumperoo has two rings on the two front suspension straps to hang toys on.  But when I got the Whoozit Activity Spiral, it turns out it is very tightly coiled, like a spring; not flexible or bendable.  It is difficult to uncoil it or to stretch it out.  And there is no mechanism to attach it to things.  Although it seems to advertise that you can wrap it around anything, this is completely not true.  There is no wire or anything inside it to attach it with.  If you feed it through a hole, it might stay.  But in the case of my jumperoo, it wouldn't stay hooked on, but just coiled back up and fell off.  So then I tried it on the baby's carseat.  Again, it wouldnt' stay on.  It was too short to wrap a coil from each end around the bars of the car seat, so it would just slide up to the top of the bar as it contracted, or it would fall right off.  IF it were made of something stretchier, or if it had wire inside that would allow you to wrap the ends around something, it might be good. I think the baby would like the little dangling toys on it.  But as it is, it won't attach to anything we have.  And when it is so tightly coiled (as it is all the time unless you're stretching it out yourself) the toys are all jumbled up in the coils and not visible or reachable for baby.  So to me it is a pretty useless toy.

This HOT WHEELS TRACK BUILDER SPIRAL STACK-UP set is a terrific choice for expanding an existing Hot Wheels track layout, as long as you have a fair amount of floor or table space.  It's a really large stunt that's about five feet long when it's assembled according to the boxed instructions (though the launcher ramps can be rearranged, using two towers instead of one, to use less space).  The way this set works, is that the first car launched from the top of the tower launches two more cars (not included with the set) as it speeds down the ramp, then triggers a release that dumps a car off the second level of the "box" located at the end of the double ramp, until it finally exits the stunt through the lower level of the "box".  The second car that is launched (by the first car) speeds down a long ramp that is connected near the top of the tower, and exits the stunt.  The third car that is launched travels down a short ramp and stops on the second level of the exit "box", waiting to for the first car to dump it.  There's a lot of action that makes me smile in this track builder set.  Also, the two cars that actually exit the setup have a lot of momentum left that will carry them elsewhere in a well-designed layout.  One of the best things about this setup is that all three cars usually complete the entire run flawlessly, without jumping the track or losing momentum or stopping too soon.  The "Nitrium" car that comes with the set is a fairly heavy one, because the set works best with a weighty car starting in the top position.  For me, this Hot Wheels playset was a little more challenging to assemble than others that I've set up.  As always, the key thing is to identify your parts correctly, and to keep looking until you find the right place to make a connection if you don't understand the instructions right away.  The color-coding of the plastic parts helped me with one confusing part--the set has two red launcher arms that attach to a yellow base, and one gray launcher lever that attaches to a gray base.  In my customer photos, there are three cars loaded into the set--the orange/white car at the very top is the included Nitrium car.

What I thought would be a fun holiday toy for my kids turned out to be a huge pain and battery guzzler. While packaged nicely, it was evident that you get a product that, in my opinion, isn't even worth the relatively low price point. The controller is made of thin plastic and has unnecessary triggers that make me wonder whether it's a generic mold that's been used for game controllers of some sort. The body of the car is a bit sturdier, at least. With the fat tires, the body hardly ever touches surfaces.  When I first took the parts out of the box the first thing I did was open up the back of the controller to put in batteries. The battery cover also has a screw to keep it in place, but I soon realized that the plastic screw (or else very cheap metal) was broken so that no matter how much I turned it it would not loosen. I didn't strip the top; the screw itself was broken. Thus, I had to bend and crack the battery cover open to get the batteries in. "Okay," I thought. "At least that'll probably be the worst of it." Nope.  I did the same for the car. Thankfully, that screw wasn't broken so it came off without any manipulation. The instructions state that a rechargeable battery is inside, but that's not the case. Instead, there's a compartment that requires four AAA batteries. I stock up on plenty of AA batteries, but as AAA batteries are becoming less common I had to order some. Not a big deal; I've been meaning to stock up on some anyway. And I actually prefer batteries over internal rechargeable ones as that means better longevity. (I have this twenty-year-old RC car that is useless now because the rechargeable battery is impossible to find a replacement for.)  So I got the batteries all set and I started to play with it. As it was late at night my kids were asleep, so I figured what better way to test it before they had a chance! The car moved fine. It detected all my presses. However, two big problems I noticed were: 1) to push the car forward, you have to push both triggers forward (or backward if the car's on its flip side), but every time I did the car would make a slight turn left before continuing forward. I tried countless times to see if it was me but it would not go straight before turning a slight left. 2) Because the underside of the car is mirrored from the top I sometimes forget that I'm supposed to reverse the controls. I wish they made the mold of the car so that they wouldn't be facing the same direction.  Anyway, I figured my kids would have fun with it and wouldn't bother with these gripes. They woke up and noticed the car immediately on the table, and I let them have at it. For a while, they had a lot of fun, but not too long did they complain that the car wouldn't go straight. When the car would stop and they'd try to get it to go just straight, sure enough it would turn slightly left before going straight. Despite their complaints, however, they used it the entire day. By the end of the day, the batteries inside the car already needed replacing. Mind you, they didn't use it every second of the day, but I was still surprised to find that the batteries needed replacing already. I changed them and thought nothing of it.  Two days later was Thanksgiving and my kids wanted to bring it while visiting family. When we got there the car needed battery replacements yet again. In those two days prior to the holiday they used it probably a max of about half an hour. There's a switch on the car that turns it off and on, but then I realized that if it's not turned off then the batteries still are being drained. I thought we'd be fine to leave the car switched on and the batteries would be in a sort of idle state, but that was not the case. As the family we visited didn't have AAA batteries, my kids weren't able to use it. It wasn't a total loss as we had plenty of other activities to do, but even I was looking forward to using the car with a couple of pets brought by family.  So when we got back home I replaced the batteries. When my kids went to bed I remembered to turn the car off. The next day they played with it and forgot to turn it off that night. The next day it needed replacements again. I told my kids I'm not replacing the batteries again and then I showed them this RC helicopter I had. They've forgotten about the car.

This ETA HAND2MIND 20-BEAD REKENREK (with Activity Guide) is a solid, beautifully made wood-and-metal counting frame.  The beads feel nice, and slide easily.  The frame is accompanied by a teaching manual that includes lessons, number “cards” to be cut out and mounted on heavier stock, and student problem worksheets for duplication.  The Rekenrek is intended to help students visualize numbers.  I found the lessons hard to follow at first—an important element is understanding that the starting “Home” position is with all the beads on the right. If taught correctly, I can see how the Rekenrek would make early math classes (K-2) more tactile and fun.  The Rekenrek isn’t expensive (about $15 on Amazon), considering how well-made it is.  However, it will cost a fair amount to equip a class for “Rekenrek math”.  One Rekenrek is required for each student, and one for the teacher.

I love the Trackmaster toys as an alternative to wooden railways that are more expensive, less detailed and not as suitable for power.  However, the design flaws and poor quality coming from Fisher Price need improvement.  In the case of the tender locomotives, the battery is carried in the tender and the motor is in the locomotive.  Only a thin plastic coupler holds them together and an electrical wire between the two will break when the coupler fails.  It's short and difficult to splice.  The result is a ruined toy simply because the child carries the locomotive around by holding one segment or the other.  My boys have a cousin with virtually every Trackmaster locomotive.  Most of the tender locs have broken couplers.  Fisher Price needs to fix this so the battery is in the engine and the tender can decouple without breaking the toy.

This flies beautifully and gracefully and is very forgiving with the AS3X technology.  It allows it to recover nicely from stalls and keeps it relatively stable even in light gusty wind, especially impressive given its light weight.  Still, it won't prevent you from crashing.  And this is where I deduct a star.  It's quite delicate.  You'll benefit from buying some foam-safe glue (Beacon FOAM TAC is a good one).  The build quality isn't too good; the parts inside aren't very well secured to the body, and I'm having some issues with a sticky elevator servo.  Other annoyances are the battery attachment to the underside with velcro isn't very secure, and the power cable often gets lost inside the body near the propeller housing (tweezers needed to pull it back out.  And as others have noted the method for attaching the front wing with screws is dicey at best; not sure when it's actually holding it in place (when the screws fall out, it isn't).  But aside from these sloppy construction details, I'd still recommend it because it's such a great flyer.  I'm using a Devo 7e transmitter running Deviation 4.0.1 which includes support for the DSM2/DSMX protocol used by the UMX Radian BNF.  No hardware changes needed to the transmitter.  11/08/2015 UPDATE:  The sticky elevator servo cause me not to be able to recover from a nosedive and the crash caused the tail section to break almost completely off. I was able to glue it back on but the flight isn't as pure as it was out of the box. I called Horizon Hobby about my issues and they have sent me a replacement UMX Radian free of charge.

We love the detailed trains in the Thomas line but these new tracks are inferior to the previous generation and most other tracks on the market.  The new tracks are lame because it uses a proprietary connector that is incompatible with the previous generation and other tracks like Brio, etc.  They do include a plastic adapter to adapt the new connector to the old style but the adapter is a poor solution to a problem that shouldn't exist in the first place.  The worst part about these new tracks isn't even the proprietary connector though, its the fact that they are all single sided so you cannot flip them over to get a different angle to make different track configurations.  That means that this set can only be configured in the figure eight loop shown in the picture unless you purchase additional track pieces with the proprietary connector.  To me the whole fun of a train set is creating your own track configurations so I am really not a fan of this update.  Another thing that is annoying is the fact that about half the pieces of this wood set are actually plastic.  Only the trains and the basic track pieces are wood, the special track pieces and buildings all use plastic.  It is sad that they would make such a poorly thought out update to a line of toys which has always been known for its attention to detail and quality.  We will continue to use the trains from this line but with other tracks which are more flexible and compatible with the older version.

LEGO MAKE YOUR OWN MOVIE (by the editors of KLUTZ) is a how-to guide for stop-motion-movie-making, using LEGO minifigures and bricks.  It’s aimed at young children, and it really hits the mark.  I think that any child who is able to read the simple instructions should be able to make his/her own LEGO movies, and that even younger children can make stop-motion movies with adult help.  The book includes LEGO parts, background scenery, and basic scripts to get the reader started with a few simple movies, using animation apps that are loaded with cell phone cameras or that are readily available online as free downloads.  My favorite feature of the book is the deck of “scene cards” that can be removed and photographed in sequence to test the performance of the reader’s chosen animation software.  If you perform this “scene card” test, you will master the basic photography skills needed, before you invest a lot of time setting up your stop-motion frames with actual LEGO pieces.  This book isn’t just for kids, either—it’s a great starter book for anyone who’s interested in learning how to create his/her own LEGO movies.

This is an unexpectedly accurate Rogue One stormtrooper helmet for the price.  The fit for an adult is a bit tight, but can be worn without a problem if you adjust the straps to extend around the full circumference of the inside of the helmet.  With a proper fit your forehead and bridge of your nose will press against the front of the helmet.  Not particularly comfortable for wearing a long time, but allows moving your head without the helmet slipping.  The recessed "gills" of the tube stripes and the tears below the corners of the eyes are detail features unique to the R1 stormtrooper done well in this design.  The voice changer does work, though it's not that loud.  This is a sturdy heavy helmet at 2.2 pounds.  The 3-piece design easily snaps together, though does leave some visible seams, but not too bad.  But for the original price at release, this is a very good deal.

***UPDATE 08/29/13*** My daughter, who is one, went to grab Elmo the other day. Elmo was sitting against the couch on the floor, and my daughter grabbed his foot to bring him to her. The head fell on the floor, obviously, but there was an audible *thunk*, and my wife and I looked at each other. I immediately went to test out the doll, and found it no longer worked. I replaced the batteries, but that did nothing.  It stinks that a simple drop from a resting position would make Elmo completely inoperable. Again, he was already sitting on the floor, so the fall wasn't that great.  Ah, well. At least my daughter can use it as a regular Elmo doll. A $50 Elmo doll with stiff arms and a hard head. ***  First thought I had: This Elmo is HUGE! My daughter is only a year old, but she's already obsessed with the little red monster. We decided to get this for her, and when we opened the Amazon box we found it to be bigger than even her!  The electronics are great and interactive, as Elmo constantly wants a hug from my daughter. Being that my daughter is not even a year old and doesn't fully understand speech and its many commands, she ignores Elmo's pleas, opting instead to hold him by his arms or legs. She'll hug him--when she wants.  The arms are fine and seem sturdy enough, but I'm afraid they won't last long time. Hopefully they'll last as long as my daughter's infatuation with the creature, but I don't expect this one staying with the family for generations.  It's a great Elmo to have. I think the high price is due mainly to the height of the figure. I've seen electronic toys with more gizmos go for cheaper, so my guess is the height and licensing.  A great Elmo toy. As if the other two my daughter had weren't enough.

As an Adult Fan of LEGO, I love the big, complicated sets like this LEGO IDEAS VOLTRON 121311 BUILDING KIT (2321 Pieces).  The LEGO building instructions are always crystal clear, so that even the biggest sets with the most unusual building techniques are easy to assemble.  All that’s required is that you set out the pieces for each step, and check your model against the picture before you go on to the next step.  This big Voltron mech is made up of five smaller transformer lions, just like in the Japanese anime TV series and the later Dreamworks series.  This is the absolute best kind of big LEGO set, because the many elements and parts extend the building experience as long as possible. When you’re done, you have a great model to display, and when you tire of the model, you have a lot of elements available to use for other projects of your own design.  Volton comes out of the LEGO Ideas program, which means it was originally submitted to the LEGO Ideas website by a LEGO fan.  After the fan model received 10,000 supporting votes from other LEGO fans, it was selected to be sold as a regular LEGO set.  The Ideas sets are generally available for a limited time, so you should order Voltron fairly promptly if you love the set.  Downloadable building instructions are always available at the LEGO website--look them up by the set number (121311 for Voltron).  However, the cheapest way to get all the necessary elements--and any specialized elements--is to buy a building set when it’s still available from LEGO.  When LEGO has a set, Amazon often has the same set at a slightly lower price.

I already like Tiny Epic games. This is probably my favorite. If you can imagine a Jurassic Park game, this is more or less the one you want. Small package, great price, endless fun. What more can you ask for?  You build parks and enclosures to house dinosaurs. The dinosaurs are hungry. If you can't feed them, they'll still eat - guess what? - your other dinosaurs! Very well made and lots of fun. If you don't like this game, maybe board games aren't your thing.  I look at this game as an example to understand how to make games well. I think anyone who likes board games has to give at least one Tiny Epic game a try, and this is the one I would recommend most.  Limited closet space? Desperate for something besides Monopoly, Risk, or Settlers of Catan? Like building games with dire consequences? Think dinosaurs are the least bit cool or interesting? Look no further.  My only minor criticism is that some might consider it complex with the different special dinosaurs and some of the rules about how and when to place dinos. Any board game is going to have a learning curve, and this one is small.

The H8C uses a 7.4v 2S battery as opposed to this one's 3.7v single cell. They're both 500mAh. So, you're getting more power and longer flying time out of the H8C. Plus, for about the same money, the H8C comes with a 2mp camera. You'd have to buy the upgraded version of the X5 (the X5C) to get a camera, and of course for more money. I like the H8C better.

I field tested this science kit on my 6 1/2 year old cousin, who is very bright and very interested in all things scientific.  He loved doing the experiments, and though some adult supervision is definitely needed, he could do most of the measuring and adding of compounds on his own. This LEGO CREATOR EXPERT VESTAS WIND TURBINE BUILDING KIT (10268; 826 Piece) is a fascinating set that’s much more fun than the advertising makes it appear.  Its best feature is also its greatest drawback--the huge turbine blades take a lot of space to display and operate.  The blades are motorized (with a servo hidden in the “Vestas” box at the top), and when they’re turning, the two house entrance lights turn on.  What better way to dramatize the point that a single huge wind turbine can power thousands of homes?  The model is slightly challenging to build, because it calls for a lot of studs-not-on-top assembly.  It’s especially interesting to see how the heavy wind turbine assemblage is secured to the base with only LEGO elements.  The set does use specialized large green elements around the shaft to hide the battery box and to help hold the wind turbine firmly.  The model also demonstrates the one feature that bothers me about wind turbines as a source of electricity--they are huge, and loom over their surrounding landscape.  The little house is dwarfed by the wind turbine.  The house is cute, with windows and door that open, a range and sink, a lamp on a table, and a patio with a table and chairs (created from “repurposed” wheel-well elements).  The minifigs (two Vestas engineers, a female homeowner, and a dog) are great.  The engineers have a van that holds their work bench and a hand truck loaded with service tanks and a big assortment of tools.  The instruction book includes several pages describing the development of wind turbines as a power source, and showing diagrams of how they work to create electricity from wind currents.  The wind turbine on the model can be rotated by hand atop the shaft.  Only the wind turbine blades are motorized.  The blades are operated by a switch at the bottom of the shaft.  The motor and lights run on 6 AA batteries (not included).  The trees in the set are made from plants.  (My LEGO Wind Turbine set was purchased from a LEGO Shop.)

Back in the early 2000's, we used to do Murder Mystery Parties with our group of friends. They were kits in boxes where everyone was assigned a character and we followed a loose script to solve the mystery. This is nothing like that. It's an upgraded version for sure. I would describe it as somewhat similar to an escape room experience, but instead of trying to escape, you are solving a murder. There are no characters to assign which is a relief! I hated trying to assign characters without offending people. And the characters were assigned genders so if you didn't have an even number of guys and girls, someone would have to play the opposite. It was never a big deal in my group of friends, but it could be for some.  Here you are the detective and team doing the solving. It comes with all kinds of fun evidence. There's a newspaper clipping that looks like a real newspaper clipping. There's a traffic ticket, photos, and so much more. I was very impressed with the level of creativity that went into it. The old style mystery boxes would have silly clues that didn't look real. This is so much cooler! If you use pencil or write out notes on your paper, you could easily pass this set onto someone else.

This KANO HARRY POTTER CODING KIT works as advertised.  It requires an app (Win10, FireOS, Android, iOS) for operation and play.  (The Kano Code app for the iPad seems to work well.)  The wand is fairly large--big enough to look and feel like a real magic wand.  The kit includes a booklet with instructions for assembling the wand.  You insert batteries into a circuit board, put a button piece and the circuit board into a part of the wand, and close the wand with another piece.  Adding the last wand piece is a bit tricky, but the finished wand looks nice and doesn’t fall apart.  A button on the end of the wand opens it up for battery changes.  Two sets of batteries are included.  The app allows a child to create a Kano account, but sends an email to a parent’s email address to allow the parent to set the child’s privileges.  The app opens to an illustrated map of the Hogwarts campus.  The map has colored spots that you tap or select to open the “challenges”.  For example, an early challenge teaches the child to change the colors on a drawing of an owl.  The coding is accomplished by dragging and dropping code blocks until they click together and add the color-changing capability to the wand.  As an adult who knows almost nothing about coding, I didn’t fully understand how the coding blocks went together or how the wand was supposed to be used once a new coding feature was added.  With a little more study time, I’m sure that I would have figured out how to do the coding.  However, if the wand is intended for a very young child, an adult with minimal coding knowledge will probably be required to help the child get started.  The coding kit also includes a nice chart of the wand motions required to execute the spells used in the Harry Potter books and movies.  This kit should keep a child busy for hours, if the child is motivated to learn to use the wand.

This LEGO SPEED CHAMPIONS 76895 FERRARI F8 TRIBUTO BUILDING KIT (New 2020 (275 Pieces)) is recommended for ages 7 and up, but very young children may need some adult help with this kit.  It takes some hand strength to add the tires to the hubs; and there are quite a few small left-oriented and right-oriented pieces that have to be placed just so, or the bodywork won’t curve correctly (see my closeup photo).  The finished Ferrari is cute, and solidly built for play.  The top comes off so that the minifig can sit inside.  There are no car doors that swing open (a feature that is standard on the big Technic car sets).  The minifig comes with a wrench, and with both a helmet and a wig (the helmet does NOT fit when the wig is on).  There are two different sets of wheel covers for the Ferrari.  The set takes an hour or two to build, if you’re not in a hurry.  As always, the printed step-by-step LEGO building instructions are very clear.  You can also get the building instructions onto a tablet or other mobile device by scanning a maze code (included in the printed instructions) and then following the directions to download the appropriate app.

It’s absolutely charming, all 6 pieces of it!  I really like the drivers’ windows, with a boy on one side and a girl on the other.  It makes this into a truly unisex toy.  I love the big blue traffic cone that fits between the drivers’ cabin and the moveable crane for transport.  Tow trucks always set out traffic cones, for safety, when they’re readying a tow.  The crane itself is fun because it swings up and down freely.  The building instructions are on the outside of the retail box.  The toy takes about 30 seconds for an adult to assemble, as the wheels are already attached to the truck base.  When the toy is finished, it makes a terrific truck for general play.  The wheels turn freely, and the truck feels solid and satisfying when it’s pushed around.  Inside the box is a printed city street layout.  The paper is very thin, so this won’t last long unless it’s glued to some sort of heavier backing.  However the street layout fits the assembled tow truck, and is a nice detail.  Note that this is a LEGO duplo model, intended for very young children.  The pieces are much larger than standard LEGO pieces.  They are compatible with other duplo building sets, but NOT with standard LEGO elements.

I work in a high school special ed classroom. The students are considered to be moderate-intellect. I took this game in to play with some of them. The game can be played with 2, 3 or 4 players. Each player gets a set of 12 "eye" cards. There are two cards of each eye color, but one is a left side of the face and the other is the right side. You have to physically pick up the panda board to shake the dice. At first I thought it was like the old classic game Trouble where you press on the dome to get the dice to move, but that's not how it works. After the dice are rolled, you find the two cards in your pile the match what shows up on the panda board. It took one of my students a while before she started "getting" that the left/ride side mattered, but my other student picked up on it fairly quickly. I ended up with a set of cards, too, and I would position it correctly and model it for the one student to copy. The first person to have the correct eye match, wins a tile. We ended up playing three full games. Both kids liked it a lot. Although they are in high school, it was very appropriate for their academic levels. There's no reading, so we weren't slowed down by that. I would definitely recommend this to other special ed teachers or people with preschoolers.

This LEGO ARCHITECTURE SKYLINES: TOKYO 21051 BUILDING KIT (New 2020 ; 547 Pieces) took me longer to assemble than it should have, because the building instructions are printed in white on a black background.  The model’s base uses black LEGO pieces, so you need very good light to decipher the white-on-black drawings at the beginning. There is quite a bit of studs-not-on top assembly, and there are some very innovative connective techniques.  Happily, the elements are printed with the graphics (no stickers to apply).  Thanks to the innovative, amazing architecture that exists in real-life Tokyo, the finished model makes a terrific display piece.  For some reason, I really like the back wall of the model, which represents Mount Fuji.  The finished model is small enough to fit easily into a bookcase or wall-mounted display case.  The instruction booklet includes photographs of the actual Tokyo buildings that are represented.  In addition, short text paragraphs (in English, French, and Spanish) explain the functions and backgrounds of the buildings.

Ravensburger puzzles are so well-made, they are always a pleasure to put together.  The pieces slip into place easily if they're correct, and only rarely do two similarly colored pieces seem to fit.  With Ravensburger puzzles, my mistakes are minimal.  I love to work difficult jigsaw puzzles, and assembling this LOFOTON, NORWAY puzzle was totally enjoyable.  The picture divides by color into several main areas (mountain, mountain range, dark sky, light sky, water, buildings, rocky outcropping, green trees), and none of the areas have a huge number of pieces.  If you take the time to sort the pieces, you can make quick work of this big, 1000-piece puzzle.  The hardest area is the green in the bottom right corner--that was a bit of a challenge, because the pieces are dark and have little or no photo detail!  The "augmented reality" portion of this puzzle is fairly simple.  After you download the "AR Puzzle" app from the iTunes store (you may have to Google it to find it), you aim your iPad or iPhone at the finished puzzle and start the app.  By tapping the screen, you get white dots of "snow", and the scene changes to a winter scene.  If you tap too much, you get a nasty and disconcerting--but harmless--little surprise!  This puzzle, although difficult, is much easier than the [[ASIN:B007ADICS2 Paris, 1000-Pieces Augmented Reality Puzzle]], which I found far less enjoyable because of the extreme difficulty of the photo.  However, the Paris puzzle has a much more elaborate and impressive Augmented Reality portion, which shows and describes the major landmarks of Paris as seen from the Eiffel Tower.  I really believe that jigsaw puzzles like these have educational value--doing jigsaw puzzles as a child helped me later with high school solid geometry and with art classes.

Hasbro’s Beyblade Burst Evolution battling tops are fun toys, but they have a learning curve.  The included instructions are so brief as to be almost useless, though they do explain how to assemble the top.  (There are detailed instructions and videos available on the toy manufacturer’s web site.  Go to the official Hasbro site, choose “Brands”, and then “Beyblade Burst”.)  The back of the toy card gives details on the features (e.g., type (attack or defense), weight, endurance, etc.) of the included top.  This BEYBLADE BURST EVOLUTION SWITCHSTRIKE STARTER PACK (Roktavor R3) contains a Roktavor 3 (a right-spin top), and a launcher that can be used for either left-spin or right-spin tops.  You will need to purchase a “battle stadium” separately (e.g., [[ASIN:B01F6VS3ZG Beyblade Burst Beystadium]]).  These tops require two people and two tops for play, because the tops are intended to be launched simultaneously for each battle.  My efforts to launch two tops in close succession weren’t very successful, and the resulting battles weren’t very satisfying.  I think that single players can use the accompanying Beyblade Burst app (available for iOS and Android).  It’s an extremely slick, easy-to-use game app that simulates stadium battles between your own Burst tops—you can scan a code from the card or from a section of the top to add the top to your collection within the app.  The parts for these Burst Evolution tops can be interchanged to create custom tops with different strengths and weaknesses.  I would strongly advise any adult who is purchasing one of these tops for a youngster to find out exactly which model it is that the kid wants.  The tops perform very differently, depending on their weight and on features such as the type of tip (e.g., rollerball).  Hasbro’s original price for these tops was apparently about $13.  Prices are higher now, and vary quite a bit among online sellers.  Fun Toy With Online Player App

You wouldn't know it from the picture, but CAFE TERRACE AT NIGHT is an extremely difficult jigsaw puzzle.  If you decide to try it, you won't need another jigsaw to work on for quite a while!  With any 1500-piece jigsaw, you have to work by general color areas.  After rough sorting, I had the fewest pieces in deep orange, so I started with them.  Because they also had the cafe tables and people, I thought they'd go together quickly.  Wrong, wrong, wrong!  Progress was so slow, I didn't want to work on the puzzle at all (even though I love doing jigsaw puzzles).  It took me about 5 weeks--working on and off (mostly off)--to finish the orange part. Now I'm working on the cobblestones, and the puzzle is going faster--because I've got the hang of Van Gogh's painting style--but it's still going much more slowly than is usual for me.  Aside from the difficulty, another problem with this puzzle is that it's very tall.  Because it's taller than my work table is wide, my original plan was to work the puzzle sideways on the work table.  I soon discovered that the puzzle is far too difficult to be assembled that way.  After a time, I turned the puzzle around so that it would be facing me straight on, and separated the top part of the puzzle border (the part that wouldn't fit on the table) to work it later as a separate puzzle section.  When both the main section and the top section are finished, I'll join them briefly for a photo session.  CAFE TERRACE AT NIGHT is a puzzle that only an expert, addicted, jigsaw puzzler will enjoy--primarily for the challenge.  Like all Ravensburger puzzles, it is manufactured to very high standards.  The pieces are made of fairly thick cardboard, and they are perfectly cut, so that the pieces slip effortlessly into place when you have the right ones.  However, because the puzzle is almost too difficult to be fun, even as an extreme jigsaw challenge, I rate it at 4 stars ("I like it" on the official Amazon scale).

[[VIDEOID:794a93ea445d6873412d11a47fb6c604]]This HOT WHEELS FAN MAN ACCESSORY is a fun addition to any Hot Wheels Track Builder set.  It adds color and motion, but not a lot of car-triggered action.  When a car  hits the lever that supposedly "flattens" the Fan Man, he pops right back up, because the trigger doesn't turn off the fan or the air flow that keeps him aloft.  (This is probably a good thing, because it means that the accessory doesn't need constant resetting.)  The Fan Man accessory is easy to assemble.  It requires two alkaline C batteries (not included) to run the fan.  You use a Phillips screwdriver to open the battery case and insert the batteries.  Then you pop the fan blade onto the blue base. Then you click the red fan guard and Fan Man assembly onto the blue base.  Then you click the orange "track holder" onto the side of the blue base.  And you're done!  The red switch turns the fan on and off.  You run a section of orange Track Builder track through the orange track holder (in any layout).  When a car comes down the track and hits the gray lever (from one direction only), the Fan Man is temporarily flattened.  My Fan Man came with a crease under one arm (from the way it was packed) but the crease smoothed out some when I blotted it carefully with water.  It will probably iron out with low heat, but I'm not going to try this, because of the danger of melting the little guy.  The crease makes no difference whatever when the Fan Man is twisting and waving his arms while the fan is turned on.

This LEGO CITY SERVICE STATION 60257 BUILDING SET (New 2020; 354 Pieces) is intended for kids aged 5 years and up, so it’s simple to build.  Of course, that means there isn’t a lot of fine detail in the vehicles and buildings, despite the high piece count.  Many of the 354 pieces are small ones, like single round studs for headlights, or a puppy figure, or a hot dog.  Still, this is a rather cool service station, because it includes a charging station for an electric SUV, in addition to gas pumps for a more traditional van.  The set includes a mini-convenience store/pay station.  The van opens up to show a spacious interior.  Both vehicles can accommodate a minifig in the driver’s seat.  There are 4 minifigs, including a station attendant.  I love all of the City sets, because they illustrate the ordinary jobs, workers, buildings, and vehicles that make up modern cities.  The City sets are easy to build, their standard LEGO elements are interchangeable with other LEGO sets (not just the City sets), and they’re relatively inexpensive.

I really like the JIMU robots, because they're interesting to build, robust when completed, and easy to drive (using the free iOS or Android app).  This UBTECH JIMU ROBOTS BUILDERBOTS SERIES OVERDRIVE KIT (410 Parts and Connectors) has everything you need to build a dump-truck robot (Dirtbot) or a dirt-mover robot (Dozerbot).  It's a great kit for people (like me) who love building models of heavy construction equipment.  If you want, you can build one model, take it apart, and build the other model.  However, be warned that the components are extremely hard to take apart.  The components are completely interchangeable with the components in other JIMU robot sets.  (The components are NOT compatible with LEGO Technic elements.) Combining JIMU sets gives you more flexibility if you want to create your own robots.  JIMU robots are controlled with Bluetooth--pairing is easy, once they're turned on or plugged into a power source.  They don't have separate controllers, so you need a tablet or smartphone to work them.  It’s a good idea to charge the controller cubes before you start building--charging takes about an hour--so that you can play with the robot as soon as you're finished building it.  Building instructions are NOT included in the box, except for a Quickstart guide that tells you where to get the detailed instructions.  You have to download the JIMU app to get the building instructions.  The detailed instructions are presented in 3D--at each step you can rotate the model 360 degrees and look at it from every angle until you’re sure that you’ve got it right.  The JIMU robots are moderately difficult to build, but older kids should be able to complete them.  Once the robots are complete, you can use the Blockly coding system to program robotic moves beyond the models’ basic built-in moves.  Blockly is part of the downloaded JIMU app.

This BRIO DELUXE BIRTHDAY TRAIN is a cute, sturdy toy, with both wooden and plastic parts.  I love the idea of using this toy as the centerpiece in a small child's birthday celebrations (ages 1-6 years).  What a nice family tradition that would be!  The train is a little large for a table centerpiece, but the large size means it will photograph well when it's displaying a child's age on his/her birthday.  The set includes wooden blocks with numbers 1 to 6, but you don't really have to stop at 6 years. You can easily draw or print the rest of the numbers you need (0,7,8,9, and duplicates of 1 through 9), tape them over the blocks, and set up as many as two number blocks in the two cars.  You can celebrate birthdays from 1 to 99!  Also, I think that a small child would have a lot of fun playing with this colorful little train.  The two cars couple and uncouple easily, and have a lot of room for loading in small toys like zoo animals or building blocks.  When the locomotive and cars are coupled together, the wheels turn freely, so the train is easy to push along a table or floor, hauling its load.

First thing worth noting is that the rifle will jam if you're not familiar with using it. And if it jams, don't expect to reuse the bullet it got jammed on. But there is a good excuse for this  That being, this rifle works a lot like a real bolt action firearm, or at least compared to the pump action rifles which I find are kinda a silly concept as pumping a rifle to get it to fire is very unrealistic.  I won't spoil how to use the gun but I can say you have to get a feel for the method of firing it before you can go running around the park like a madman shooting it at random children(EG: not a toy for kids who can't get past the rifle's complexity. get them a gun that works similar to the night finder if they are too young to figure out stuff like this or have low attention spans).  Now getting passed getting used to the gun, it's hella entertaining to use. whether you use the stock and fill it with two quick reload clips to give you extra ammo, or you go with the barrel extension and the flip grenade launcher style sight, or if you're a madman like me you go without the stock or the barrel and just go rambo with it using a drum magazine(this is definitely a nerf gun you'll want extensions from other nerf guns on, by the way), you are bound to have hella fun times with it.  Just don't put the stock in unless you want to keep it in for a while, as the only way to get it out is too unscrew the back of the gun and lift it out.  Now, the down side to the gun is that it has a weak range for a "sniper," which is estimated 25-35 feet, depending on if you use the barrel extension or not. However, a cheap way to bring that up to 30-40 feet is to tape down the unjamming door and air release thingies on the sides of where the clips go with clear household tape. This will give the gun more pressure and make this a very very good mid range rifle. That mixed with the realistic feel of a real rifle, it's definitely worth it for the older, more dedicated nerf fans.  Bottom Line: Pros: -Realistic feeling that requires a more professional handing. -Good for customization with other nerf accessories. -Overall quality mid range nerf rifle.  Cons: -Hard on casual/beginner users and those who have very little respect for the toy (EG not for little kids) -Can really mess with ammo if you jam the gun. -Stock is semi permanent and you're technically not supposed to remove the stock anyways. (Best to decide before you put it in if you want it to have a stock or not) -Shorter range then marketed for. (I wouldn't hold this against the toy itself as everything is over marketed these days. The rifle is for midrange and is meant to be realistic, it's not supposed to be a super sniper that you can running around firing the bullets 70 feet away from you without going into an ark. Not even the Elite rifles do that.)  Disclaimer: I do not recommend that the people who buy this product do modifications to the gun that require opening it or removing screws. The only modification I recommended was the one about putting tape over the external holes that lead to where the bullets go, which does not involve any tampering of the hardware internally and hence does not count as an illegal modification. I hope any moderators understand I have no intent of breaking Hasbro or Amazon regulations. Thank you.

One of my sons has an Redcat Everest 10 and it's been good and durable for 2 years now.  My other son worked hard to get this Gen7.  Unfortunately, the plastic "steering arms" were cracked.  We ordered a replacement and after a few days he received it.  The car can go very fast compared to an Everest 10, so I installed mechanical throttle limiters (hot-glued hex-nuts) on the remote so it could not be pushed or squeezed to full throttle in reverse or forward.  By limiting speed, we were hoping to keep the car in-tact.  Max speed was set a little faster than an Everest 10.  Still, within 10 minutes a front wheel fell off.  The steering arm had torn off.  It's held on by two screws and the screws tore through their hole on the plastic steering arm.  I can see Redcat sells upgraded aluminum steering arms for twenty-four dollars, but I'm concerned if I spend on those, the next weakest link, maybe the axles or a gearbox, will bend or break instead.  I've built real 4x4's and I know you don't just upgrade a part that keeps breaking without engineering the system to work together.
