I absolutely love dolls of all types and own hundreds of them. They range from inexpensive off-brand play dolls, legitimate BJDs, to high-end handmade artist dolls. This is likely the worst designed doll I have ever handled.  This doll has a very cute face and poses well. However, the deal-breaker is that she has No Neck! Her head sits right on her shoulders. This is very distracting because it makes it hard to look at her. I would be able to overlook so many of the other negative aspects to this doll if she had a neck like the doll in the description photos. This is very disappointing. It's such a pity as she has a very pretty face.  This is not a BJD (ball-jointed doll). This doll shares a similar aesthetic to some BJDs, but she does not have even a single ball joint. She has hinge and swivel joints only. The doll is made from at least three different types of plastic. One maybe ABS plastic, but there are no silicone components to this doll.  Her hair is pretty rooted plugs. The description led me to believe it may be a wig, but no.  The plugs are evenly spaced and I do not see any bald spots. The hair fibers are soft. The eyelashes are glued strips and the eyes are inset acrylics. She has very pretty face that is painted symmetrically. The joints were very, very stiff and difficult to loosen with force. Once loosened, she does pose decently and can touch her face. She has a full outfit that is made with flawed fabric. Several threads are missing in the weave of the fabric of the skirt. The flawed fabric is very noticeable. The outfit has a shirt, vest, skirt, bloomers, knee socks and a hat. It would be a very cute outfit if the stitching was a little more straight and the fabric was not deeply flawed fabric. The material is incredibly low quality. Her shoes are light purple canvas, do not match the outfit, but are fair quality and fit her flat feet snugly.  A packet of 'accessories' are included and is confusing. They are thin plastic, various shades of pink and not to scale with the doll. The comb and 4 little hair elastics may be useful to play with the doll but otherwise they would likely not be much fun for playing with the doll and I feel would be better left out of the package. Some heeled shoes were also included in the packet and although I could put them on the doll, the straps were useless and they looked odd as she has flat feet. Little fingers would likely become frustrated trying to fasten the straps.  I looked into the UCanaan brand after ordering this doll. I was disappointed to learn that the company uses re-casted parts from legitimate BJD makers without permission. This is very sad/bad news. I would have expected better from a US retail site and find it deeply disappointing. I do not know that any pieces are made from the re-casted parts from other companies for this particular doll, but it's a serious negative point that should be noted. I cannot say with confidence this is not a re-casted doll. I would not be surprised to learn that it is.  I cannot recommend this doll. The primary reasons being she has no neck, is not as described and the company does shady business practices. This makes me very uneasy as it sets up customers for some serious disappointment. If you are looking for a budget-friendly legitimate BJD, there are other companies that make very nice lower-cost dolls. It takes a minimum amount of looking off the site to find them. If you are looking for a nice doll for display, or posing and play, please note this one has no neck and uses poor quality materials. Your money would be better spent elsewhere. I do not find this to be a good value in the least. She would be enjoyable for display if she had a neck like the doll in the description photos. I hope that this can be remedied in the future. I find this doll very disappointing and it's such a pity as she would otherwise be very cute with an alternative outfit.  I do hope you have found my honest review helpful. If you do, please click the Helpful button below. Thank you for reading my review.

Our kids love cutie cars, they have several of the sets and many of the little cars.. so this van which doubles as a display case seemed like a great idea.  Unfortunately it has several major flaws which makes it not very fun to play with and also a poor display case.  The main flaw is that the little wheel cutouts for the cars do not hold the cars in place securely at all, so if you arrange all your cars in a cute display, the tiniest push will have them falling all over the inside of the van in disarray.  They should have made little compartments or something to hold them in place better, the system they have is useless unless you are a collector who puts it on a shelf and never touches it.  Second, because the van is designed primarily as a display case, it has odd proportions and really tiny wheels which make it not roll very well, so it is not fun to play with if you aren't using it as a display.. overall just a really poor design, which is kind of surprising because cutie cars in general seem to be well made and thoughtfully designed.

This THAMES & KOSMOS THE BIG ENGINEERING MAKERSPACE SCIENCE EXPERIMENT KIT is the real deal--a fun model-building kit that painlessly teaches basic concepts of physics (e.g., thermal air currents, levers, momentum).  Each model (e.g., crane, helicopter) actually works, and is accompanied by a “what's happening here” explanation in the manual of why it works (e.g., turning a crank hoists a crane load by exerting force on a rope threaded through pulleys that share the weight to be lifted; a helicopter’s fast tail rotor produces a force that counteracts the main rotor’s force to stabilize the helicopter).  The models are assembled from plastic parts (some parts are similar to LEGO Technic elements) by following clear step-by-step diagrams (similar to LEGO building instructions).  They are powered by a pressurized air tank controlled by a switch with a lever.  There are no batteries to buy or charge, because you pressurize the air tank by pumping air into it (the pump assembly resembles a bicycle tire pump).  Each model has suggestions for finding out more about the scientific principles that relate to it.  The manual indicates the difficulty level of each model with a three-dot system (easy, medium, difficult).  This kit may seem expensive (about $90 on Amazon), but it teaches a LOT about the practical application of physical and engineering principles, for the price of a single medium-sized LEGO Technic kit.  It's a kit to use in home schooling, or to give to a child who really wants to understand why and how construction equipment, vehicles, watercraft, aircraft, and cameras work in the real world.

[[VIDEOID:8d5da775f41ae0104b94b3b2e1f31605]] The Ravensburger COLORFUL ACTIVITY AT TIMES SQUARE jigsaw puzzle is a lot of fun to assemble.  There are 1000 pieces, but the puzzle goes together fast, because of the many vertical lines (e.g., buildings, billboards), written advertising messages (e.g., Kodak, Marriott), and distinctive color areas (e.g., beer bottle, taxicabs,  window patterns).  It took me two days to complete.  (I'm admittedly rather obsessed once I get started on a jigsaw puzzle.)  The "augmented reality" add-on is fun to play with.  You download the free "AR Puzzle" app (available only for iPhone 3GS, 4, 4S; or iPad 2, 3) from iTunes; start the app; select your puzzle; and choose "360 degrees" or "facts".  You then activate the augmented reality display by aiming your iPhone or iPad camera at the finished puzzle (or at the complete full-color puzzle photo that's packed in the box).  If you choose "360 degrees", your device will display a view of Times Square.  The view changes as you turn yourself and your device around in a circle, so that the app seems to put you right there in the middle of the Times Square scene.  If you choose "facts", you can click on the "i" balloons as they come up over your puzzle, and get factoid displays.  (You must be patient and sweep over the puzzle photo to get the fact balloons).  My accompanying video slide show shows what you can expect from the augmented reality app on an iPad2.  My only complaint about this puzzle is that the photos of Times Square don't seem to be very current, judging from the taxicabs and the things being advertised on the billboards.  However, it's a very satisfying puzzle to assemble, and the add-on feature is interesting and easy to use.  I thoroughly enjoyed every minute I spent playing with this.

This LEGO STAR WARS POE DAMERON’S X-WING FIGHTER 75273 BUILDING KIT (New 2020; 761 Pieces) reproduces Poe’s fighter from “The Rise of Skywalker”.  The finished model is the same as Poe’s fighter from “The Force Awakens”, as reproduced in the earlier, now-retired [[ASIN:B00WHZ6WOO 75102 LEGO set]], except for changes in the color scheme, minifigs, and accessories.  The current kit uses a white and orange scheme; the earlier kit uses a black and orange scheme. I strongly prefer the dramatic black-and-orange model, but I suppose the color schemes are true to the fighters’ colors in the actual movies.  Both kits include a Poe Dameron minifig.  The current kit adds a minifig for Janna and one for a Knight of Ren.  The current kit has an R2D2 droid; the earlier kit has a BB8 droid.  The fighter is fairly easy and fast to build.  It’s a bit boring, because the four wings have many parts, but are quite similar in construction.  The model shoots studs from alongside the cockpit, and bolts from beneath the wings.  (This model strikes me as being more dangerous to the eyes than most of the bolt-shooting models, because the position of the launchers makes it hard to load the bolts without looking straight at the business end of the bolts.  Kids should maybe wear goggles when playing with this model.)  When the model is properly constructed, the wings fold flat for non-combat flight, ground maintenance, and storage; and open into their combat X-configuration when you shift a lever from the rear to the front of the fuselage.  (The lever locks into place--if yours doesn’t, just check the triple-armed connectors between the wings.  One of my connectors was dislodged from a rod during the build, so that the lever didn’t lock.  Once the connector was reaffixed, the build became rock-solid.)  The landing gear folds up flat for flight.  If you download the LEGO BUILDING INSTRUCTIONS app or the LEGO LIFE app, you can scan the Maze code on the cover of the instructions booklet and easily download the building instructions for this kit to a mobile device.  (The apps are available for iOS or Android, at the App Store or Google Play, respectively.)  The downloaded instructions are the same as the printed ones--there are no 360-degree views available, as with some of the newest LEGO sets.  A reason to download the apps, is that they will keep a record of the sets you own.  If you ever need to know whether you purchased a particular set, or if you mislay the instructions and need them again for a rebuild, you will be able to retrieve that information easily.

Even for jigsaw puzzle fanatics, this Ravensburger "Vintage Vacations" puzzle is deliciously difficult!  At first glance, it looks easy, because there are 10 different travel posters shown, and the poster scenes are separated by straight black lines.  However, the sheer number of pieces converts this "easy" puzzle into a challenging one.  It took me nearly 7 hours just to sort the 2000 pieces (by color) to my satisfaction.  Bear in mind, this puzzle is HUGE.  The finished size is 29-1/2" by 38-1/2", which means you need a large work table or dining room table to assemble it comfortably.  Because I have limited space, I put it together on a work table that was just large enough to hold the finished puzzle.  Thus I had to work from trays of sorted puzzle pieces (disposable plastic dinner and salad plates will do), and this slowed me down.  It took me about 10 days, working half days, to complete the puzzle.  To my mind, jigsaw puzzles are highly educational.  They teach shape and color recognition, and mental rotation of shapes.  With this particular puzzle, the assembly goes faster when you pick up its logic:  one travel photo has bathers in a pool, another has bathers on a beach; one photo has motor tours, another has a power boat race; two photos show city tourist spots; and so on.  The Ravensburger puzzle is high quality, as advertised.  Each piece is unique as to color and shape, and the pieces are totally interlocking--no pieces have those annoying curved edges that just slide up against each other.  The detail of the tiny people is amazing.  Also, the puzzle has airplanes, boats, clouds, and different colors that break up the larger areas of sky and water, so that the puzzle never seems impossible or boring.

With only 44 pieces, this LEGO FRIENDS STEPHANIE’S PLAY CUBE Building Kit (New 2020 (44 Pieces)) isn’t much of a building kit, but the finished project is a cute, portable toy that has a lot of play potential.  I love it because I love dollhouses and miniatures.  When you open the finished cube, chef Stephanie is there in her bakery/kitchen, alongside a beautifully decorated cake. (Decorating the cake is a fun part of the build.)  She has cupcakes in the oven, and white icing is cooking on the stove.  Her little dog is there, too, watching the whole operation.  There are really two builds in the cube, one in each half.  Both can be pulled out of the cube and placed on a table or countertop for play.  The oven door opens so you can remove the cupcakes, and the drawer underneath the cake holds extra decorations (flowers and bows).  The background of the right half of the cube is supplied by a sticker that shows an apron hanging up, and a kitchen stool, among other things.  Only two stickers (the background just mentioned, and the oven door detail) need to be applied to LEGO elements as part of the “story”.  There are extra stickers to be used however the builder chooses.  Something that I love about this play cube series, is that the “surprise” for each set is always the same animal, but in one of four different colors.  In this set 41401, the animal is a dog that may be red, gold, teal, or lavender.  I like my red dog, and am glad there is no real pressure to “collect them all“ when it comes to the "surprise”, because it’s only a matter of which color dog you get.

This LEGO DUPLO TOWN POLICE STATION 10902 BUILDING KIT (38 Pieces) is an inexpensive building set intended for very young children (age 2+ and up).  The DUPLO sets contain large building blocks that children with developing motor skills can handle--they are NOT compatible with standard LEGO sets.  This set adds a police station to the DUPLO Town series.  The station is a shallow “storefront”, but the jail cell has a little toilet, which is a nice touch.  The cell door opens and closes, so that the crook can be locked up.  The police car has a special brick for lights and a siren sound (activated by pressing a button--no batteries required).  I don’t understand why the station has a “toll gate” bar, but it raises and lowers for extra play.  Perhaps it is the opening to the police station’s impound lot.  I like the surveillance camera detail on the outside of the station.  The police car has room for two of the figures to be seated inside.  All of the “police station” details--the surveillance camera, the donuts, the uniforms--are printed on the DUPLO blocks.  (No annoying stickers for little hands to struggle with!)  The light/sound brick makes this set kind of special.  The set includes a toy police car, two police officers, a crook, a big coffee mug, and decorated bricks.

I opened the box and was pretty surprised at how few items came in this kit. You get 2 plastic cups (think red party cup, only clear, and smaller), a small scoop, 3 condiment cups (plastic, disposable cups you get for salsa at the burrito place), one lid for the condiment cups, super cheapo tiny plastic volcano, a ring pop ring (without the pop), a wooden skewer, a single pipe cleaner, a baggy of borax powder, a baggy of epsom salt, a tiny gummy bear sized polymer dolphin, a baggy of salt, 5 pH strips (and mine looked like they've already been wet), a baggy of "snow powder" (sodium polycrylate), and a tiny baggy of teensy-weensy polymer spheres. Oh! And "recipe cards".  There is only enough stuff in your box to do the experiment once. Plus, you have to supply all the other stuff (like a bowl, scissors, fruit juice, unflavored gelatin, gallon sized baggies, evaporated milk, gloves, rock salt, dry ice, baking soda... you get the picture). Several of the experiments listed don't even have anything used out of the kit! One is just a recipe for chocolate cake! All of these experiments can be found on the internet for free.  Don't buy this. The included ingredients/supplies are easily and cheaply acquired.

This LEGO IDEAS OLD FISHING STORE BUILDING KIT (21310) is about the same size (12” high, 9” wide) as the big Creator Expert town buildings. This LEGO STAR WARS “KYLO REN’S TIE FIGHTER” BUILDING KIT (75179; 630 Piece) produces a very large model, which automatically makes it one of my favorite LEGO sets.  The larger the model, the longer it takes to build, and the more detail emerges as the pieces go together.  During the build, it’s always interesting to see how the LEGO designers solve construction and appearance problems (e.g., attaching parts horizontally, or making surfaces appear to be rounded when using straight-sided bricks and plates).  In this particular model, I love the included minifigs (Kylo Ren, a First Order TIE Pilot, a First Order Stormtrooper, and a BB-9E droid).  The two working, spring-loaded shooters make the model fun to play with.  As always, the LEGO pieces are precisely made, and snap tightly together. The step-by-step assembly instructions are easy to follow, and result in a solid build.  The model is fairly heavy when complete, because it contains a lot of bricks!  The pieces are standard LEGO components, so they can be used to build other projects whenever you get tired of the model.

I am overall satisfied with my order of the Simba Steffi Love Camper Set. This arrived very well packaged for shipping. It was not overly difficult to remove from the box as most of the securing straps simply untied. There were several pieces of clear tape that needed to be removed. I do like that this set included a Steffi Love doll. It's an overall nice doll with articulated ankles and knees. I do wish that the elbows were also articulated, but they are not. She has shoes, a simple dress and sunglasses. She has underpants imprinted on her plastic pelvis. I do not advise to remove the elastic on her hair as the plugged hair will show the widely spaced hair plugs and be less than attractive.  The first impression of the playset inside the packaging is that it's very cute and attractive. The camper itself is not as durable as I had hoped. This will not stand up to rough play. I would not at all advise this to be accessible to toddlers or careless children because if one tried to use it as a seat or step-stool, it would break easily. The wheels are held in place with a metal rod, this would be a safety hazard if it came out of place. The many small accessories are also not appropriate for very small children as they could pose a choking hazard. There is not enough leg room in the two front seats for the doll's legs, they must be bent to the side or the doll has to sit sideways. Not all of the doors are well-molded to close completely. Some stickers are included, which is nice as one side of the camper already is decorated with stickers, leaving the opposite side blank. Several packets of accessories are included so the dolls can use props for various activities such as cooking, lounging and even showering. The small accessories are the best point of this playset.  I personally do not find this camper set to be a good value at it's current price point. There are too many issues that will lead to frustration in play. Primarily, the type of plastic material used to mold the camper is prone to failure as it's not at all durable. The play patterns of most small children will prove to be the source of frustration or failure of the toy. This type of plastic is prone to shattering and can produce sharp edges if broken. For many children, this will be a short-lived disposable toy. This would be most ideal for children that play gently and are able to keep small toys well organized and enjoy playing with fashion dolls. The camper itself measures about 22" long, 11" wide and 9 1/2" tall. This will need a significant amount of storage space, and would not easily withstand being stored stacked with other toys in a toybox. This would be best 'parked' under a bed, in a closet or on a shelf between play sessions to avoid a tripping hazard. I love the Trackmaster toys as an alternative to wooden railways that are more expensive, less detailed and not as suitable for power.  However, the design flaws and poor quality coming from Fisher Price need improvement.  In the case of the tender locomotives, the battery is carried in the tender and the motor is in the locomotive.  Only a thin plastic coupler holds them together and an electrical wire between the two will break when the coupler fails.  It's short and difficult to splice.  The result is a ruined toy simply because the child carries the locomotive around by holding one segment or the other.  My boys have a cousin with virtually every Trackmaster locomotive.  Most of the tender locs have broken couplers.  Fisher Price needs to fix this so the battery is in the engine and the tender can decouple without breaking the toy.  I still recommend Trackmaster, just get the tender locs that do not have "talking" features or RC so the battery will be in the engine.  Those locs will be durable as long as they are made properly from the start.  FP has quality issues with their China factory too.

Coming from an Irish family, I have accumulated many different types of Irish collectibles over the years: stuffed animals, Beanie Babies, glasses, books, jewelery...all with some sort of Irish theme. But until recently I didn't have any Irish dolls, porcelain or otherwise. I have been collecting Dolls of the World Barbies for a number of years, and more recently Princess of the World dolls, so I couldn't believe it when I found out that a Princess of Ireland Barbie would be made. I was nervous that the doll wouldn't "feel" Irish enough. So when I received her in the mail I was pleasantly surprised that she was even more beautiful than in the photos online. Her complexion is very fair, and her auburn hair is stunning in natural light. Her green eyes are sparkling and mysterious, and she wears a very light rose eyeshadow that makes her beautiful eyes stand out even more. Her costume is sort of a medieval/Celtic blend. She wears a velvety soft forest green cloak with a golden Tara brooch clasp, a traditional Celtic design. Her dress is a crinkly olive green fabric with a stripe of Celtic designs down the middle. At her waist she wears a belt of three golden medallions with Celtic designs, and on her head, perfectly balanced atop her auburn hair, a golden crown fit for an Irish princess. The box she comes in is also perfect for displaying her in. Appropriately enough, it is green (Ireland is actually a Viking word for green) with golden trim. The backdrop inside of the box is an Irish castle. There is information about the Celts on the back of the box. This is sure to become a treasured keepsake in my family, and I'm planning on buying more for relatives and friends.

I've been on the search for a smartwatch that meets all my needs. I'd like to find one that has a small watch face, bands that can be changed, uses a good app, and can monitor O2, sleep, and a wide variety of exercise. This watch was a perfect 10 in looking nice for my small wrists, and bands that can be changed. Unfortunately it fell quite short of the mark for everything else I needed.  The app has me ready to put this watch back in the box and try something else. It's a really clunky app compared to others I've tried (Fitbit, Da Fit). The exercise feature is very limiting and doesn't account for any indoor exercise like a stationary bike, aerobics, treadmill, etc. Most of the time the steps on the app are wrong compared to the steps on the watch, even after trying to sync it again. It's disappointing that there's so few watch faces to choose from. I can't even choose the screen analog look watch face that is displayed on the box, it's not an option on the app at all. The O2 feature is nice, and so is the sleep feature. I do hope they'll continue to work on the app, it does have potential but doesn't work well for me as it is right now.  I'm also having some trouble trying to use the long press on the watch itself to select features. It's just not working no matter how often I try. It's really disappointing that such a beautiful watch has so many problems. I would have really liked to use it as my main watch, but my search will have to continue on.

This has been so disappointing. All I can say is read carefully their list of compatible devices because they mean it. If it's not on that list, it's not going to work. Usually, a list of compatible devices are just a guideline and apps are programmed to be very versatile. Not this app! We have a few iPads in the house, an older iPad which is stuck on IOS 10, so you can't even download the app. But we also have an iPad Air 2 running iOS 14.4, so we figure this one satisfies the iOS 13 and up requirement. No it does not. The Arpedia app will run, but there is NO SOUND.  Then I noticed on their list, they don't list Air 2, they only list iPad Air 3. So not only does it have to be running iOS 13 and up, it has to be a specific model as well. I asked about this audio issue in the Q&A, and their answer was simplistic. They say my DO NOT DISTURB might be turned on. However, I checked and it is NOT turned on. If that's the case, my other apps wouldn't have audio either, but they are fine. It is only the Arpedia app that has no audio.  On the Android side, usually apps are programed to run on Android, or not, PERIOD. You either make it work for Android, or you don't. I rarely encounter apps, such as Arpedia, that requires a specific model or brand. So again, you should look at the compatibility list and take the list seriously. We have several Android devices, we found one that worked, a Winnovo P20, but the video is UPSIDE DOWN. Turning on or off the auto rotate in the settings didn't help. If we want the video on screen to be right side up, then we have to flip the book on the table upside down. If we want the book to be right side up, the the video on screen will be upside down. LOL! We also tried several Amazon Fire HD that have the Playstore and google services sideloaded. We get the app to launch, and it will go through the process of recognizing and downloading the book you want to read, but then when it comes time to launch the book, the app crashes.  So basically if you have the latest and greatest products such as Samsung, or newest iPads, you're probably good to go. Everyone else, you can skip this product. It's not ready for a wider audience. Too bad because the materials look interesting, and my kids were just waiting on the sidelines for Dad to make it work, but I couldn't.

Mini hair ties are a $1 at dollar stores (see more info below)  Review: For use with wooden train sets (Thomas, Maxim, Brio, Melissa and Doug, etc).  This actual product does a good job of keeping the pieces together and cuts down toddler frustration.  But the bands are thick and hard to manipulate.  They're impossible to get back off and equally as impossible for a young child to use.  They don't give you the "floating through the air" kind of track support as pictured, but they do a great job of keeping pieces that get bumped out of place easily.  ALTERNATIVE #1: Your local dollar store sells little mini hair ties (for use one little girls hair... they're a little smaller than the size of a dime).  I picked up 500 clear bands for a dollar!!!!  Sure, I have to twist two or three around instead of one.  But I actually prefer them to the Snug Trac because they are easily to twist around the ends, they're easy to take back off and I can layer them on depending on how much support I need.  ALTERNATIVE #2:  We also bought the "Suretrack" track supports, which are able to provide the "floating through the air" type track set ups.  So yeah, I'd recommend the dollar store ties and the Suretrack set and NOT these overpriced rubber bands.

I love LEGO building kits, but the LEGO Star Wars figures are NOT my favorites.  I prefer the regular Technic and Creator kits, which have fewer specialty parts that can't be used for anything else.  This LEGO STAR WARS “REY” BUILDING KIT (75528; 85 Pieces) takes about half an hour to assemble.  It produces a posable figure with a rather cool Lightsaber arm that moves up and down when you twist a control at the back of the figure.  Rey’s head looks great, and her body is printed with nice armor and cross-draped fabric.  However, every pose must be done with care, because the ball joints are stiff and the armor is clipped vertically onto ball connectors.  There's no good place to hold the body when you reposition the arms and legs, so it's easy to knock pieces off, or to pull the figure apart.  The kit uses various Technic connectors, so it helps to be familiar with these when you assemble the figure.  (The kit is slightly harder to assemble than a kit with traditional bricks.)  As always with LEGO kits, it's interesting to see how the specialty parts all combine into a final build that looks very different from the individual pieces.

This GLISTELLO 1000-PIECE JIGSAW PUZZLE FOR ADULTS - DE HAAR CASTLE, NETHERLANDS (27.5 x 19.7 inches) is a good quality puzzle, highly appropriate for jigsaw puzzle experts.  It has many similar cloud and tulip pieces that present a challenge to assembly, because the pieces need to be placed by shape as well as by color.  Of course, jigsaw puzzle experts love these difficult jigsaws!  The puzzle pieces are small, which makes the 1000-piece puzzle fit easily on a worktable.  The fully interlocking pieces are thick enough to be handled easily, and the interlocking tabs don’t bend if the pieces are handled with ordinary care. The pieces don’t click together like Ravensburger puzzle pieces, but they fit together unmistakably by feel when properly joined.  Unfortunately, the pieces don’t fit tightly enough to allow joined sections to be picked up and moved without falling apart.  Completed sections need to be picked up on a sheet of paper, or pushed along the tabletop, to reach their proper locations in the puzzle.  The puzzle picture looks exactly like the photograph on the product page.  The picture is nicely printed on the puzzle itself, and on a big sheet packed inside the box for reference during assembly.  The price (about $19 on Amazon) is very reasonable for a jigsaw puzzle of this size and quality.  I wouldn’t recommend this puzzle for group assembly, because of the many pieces that are visually similar in color and shape.  Puzzles like this one require a lot of sorting of pieces in advance by color and shape if the actual assembly is to proceed quickly.

They all know and like the original game.  The directions to the game are easy to understand.  The point of the game is to destroy the other player's fleet of ships.  There is no difference between the Star Wars version and the original version except that some of the pieces are characters or ships from Star Wars.  You could enjoy this game even if you had no previous knowledge of Star Wars, but it would appeal to you more if you were a SW fan. Still, if you like Star Wars, you would definitely have fun with this version of Battleship.

These wood flip tops work just as well as the metal version from the videos I've seen.  Plus you get 4 of these for about half the price.  And you can spin them on any flat smooth surface and not worry about scratching the surface.  These are milled smooth and have a nice wood smell, no paint smell.  They are colored with dye with a light urethane finish.  I noticed the tops of these are flat whereas the metal ones are not.  So as an experiment, I sanded around the top edge of one, into a curve tapering to a point rather than flat top, thinking this is the way most tops are.  But this didn't work - it would not spin for more than a second after it flipped.  But this actually makes sense.  It needs to have a sheared flat top because the center of gravity (COG) is not straight up and down as it's spinning.  It's the perimeter edge that lines up better with the offset vertical COG when it's inverted.  I sanded it back down flat and it works fine.  I call this one "Stubby."

When I was a kid, I had one of these fashion plate toys. It was fun. You set the outfit, rub the crayon over it, then color it. So, although the primary purpose of this toy is vanity, I like the creative and artistic aspect of it. My 6 year old daughter was thrilled when she saw it. The packaging leaves much to be desired. It is a clamshell package. I took it out, showed her how it worked, and set her to work on creating some outfits. There are 3 bottom plates (6 options), 3 top plates (6 options), and 3 head plates (6 options).  All the plates fit inside a little drawer. It comes with one crayon to rub with an attachment so you don't have to get your fingers all messy and 4 colored pencils.  This is super cheap quality. The lid only closes (on the top) when you have the drawer pushed in. The plates themselves are not exactly even, they are a bit warped and don't lay flat. The drawing area is too small for a regular sheet of paper, but slightly larger than half a sheet of paper. You cut paper in half to use half sheets and they slide around a bit in the top area. Since the plates are a bit warped, you can't get a good rubbing of the pictures. (One skirt and top is polka dotted and not even half the dots will transfer to the paper.) The top is difficult to open. It also easily pops off on the opposite side that should not be opening. The eyes usually don't transfer to the rubbing either.  The colored pencils it comes with are pretty poor quality and not good color choices. (Brown, indigo, light pink, and yellow.) They don't have very much pigment in them and you're coloring on top of a rubbing, so you need much more vivid pencils than it comes with.  Within about 20 minutes of use, my 6 year old had already found the duct tape and taped the lid down. She likes the product and said other girls would like it too, just use duct tape.

This HOT WHEELS CRISS CROSS CRASH TRACK SET is a sure-fire child-pleaser, because the whole point of it is to set up vehicle crashes and near crashes.  Strangely, there is only ONE Hot Wheels car included with this "crash-producer" set (although inexpensive, compatible, standard Hot Wheels cars are readily available at supermarkets and drug stores).  The setup is simple--just connect the track sections to the main launcher section in four wide loops.  There are no twisting spirals or complicated loops to assemble "just so", because the battery-powered launcher supplies the power that keeps the cars moving along the tracks until they crash.  The launcher requires four "D" (standard flashlight) batteries (batteries NOT included).  The track in this set is the same track that is used in standard Hot Wheels gravity-car layouts.  The launcher can be connected to other standard track sets (in the same fashion as the many other Hot Wheels "stunt" sets), so it's a nice choice for expanding children's existing Hot Wheels track layouts.

This MEGA BLOKS CALL OF DUTY ANTI-AIRCRAFT VEHICLE has a lot of interesting detail, but it's quite a bit smaller than I expected.  The small size requires small blocks, and to stay in scale the soldiers' accessories (e.g., the hand grenade, the pockets, the belts) get really tiny.  Thus the assembly is more difficult than for other building toys with similar numbers of pieces.  The camouflage coloring on most of the blocks also slowed my assembly down, because it makes the blocks harder to see and identify.  (But of course that's what camouflage is supposed to do, and you want camouflage in a military-themed building toy!)  The soldiers are indeed "superposable" action figures, per the claim on the box.  The figures bend at the knees and elbows; and the head, torso, arms and legs all attach with ball joints, so they can move in many directions.  The instruction booklet (4-3/4" x 8-1/8") is pretty good, though some of the pages have a few too many of the assembly steps combined.  It's easy to make mistakes when blocks are added on the top and the bottom of other blocks in a single step.  Because I found the toy overly difficult to assemble on account of the small size, I rate it at 4 stars ("I like it" on the official Amazon scale).

This LEGO STAR WARS KESSEL RUN MILLENIUM FALCON BUILDING KIT  (75212; 1414 pieces) is a great set—definitely one of my all-time favorites.  When completed, the kit produces a large, heavy but solid, fun-to-play-with model of the “Kessel Run” Millennium Falcon that’s featured in the new Han Solo movie (“Solo:  A Star Wars Story”, released on 5/24/2018).  As an adult, I really love this Millennium Falcon, which has a large removable space shuttle in front that accommodates a minifigure, and two powerful, spring-loaded bolt shooters.  The bolts always fly 18” or more (you get four bolts).  There is also a gunnery pod for minifigures in the center circular area.  The build is fairly demanding, which makes it a lot of fun for experienced builders.  The parts you assemble aren’t always symmetrical, and there are a lot of left- and right-handed components, so you have to pay attention to the instructions as you build.  There are some “studs not on top” segments, and some components (like the removable transparent nose cone) that are attached “sideways” to vertical studs.  There are also a few items (e.g., the lever-action doors at the top front) that attach with Technic parts.  Most of the “triangular” pieces on the top of the model swing up for easy access to the furnished interior.  Inside there are chairs that accommodate minifigures, control panels (done with stickers), coffee bars, and engine parts storage boxes.  The stickers aren’t excessive, and are easy to position.  (I use a thin, flat, metal spudger to pick up and place the stickers accurately.)  The set has several specialized bricks that I haven’t seen before (e.g., the bricks that form a channel for the space shuttle to slide in and out of the front of the model).  It also has some unusual building techniques that are used to link together pieces that are installed along a curved line.  The model has four sturdy feet, which allow it to stand on a flat surface with space for the underbody gun turret.  The set has only 1414 pieces, but many pieces are very large (e.g., the triangles that open on top).  The finished model is a big, substantial toy (the circle is 12” across).

This RAVENSBURGER VIEW OF TOWER BRIDGE JIGSAW PUZZLE (1000 Piece) is a great “quick” project for a jigsaw puzzle expert.  It took me about 2½ days, working several hours at a stretch in four or maybe five extended sessions. (I get kind of compulsive about jigsaw puzzles, always hunting for “just one more piece” before stopping.)  The puzzle consists of five main parts: the water and tower bases (easy because of the colors); the tower decks, suspension cables, and pedestrian walkways (easy because of the colors, patterns, and straight lines); the towers (moderately difficult because there are two similar towers); the autumn leaves (hard, because there are a lot of similarly-colored pieces with “abstract” patterning); and the sky (hard, despite the clouds, because there are a lot of pieces to place by subtle color differences and by shape).  These different sections draw on all of an expert's skills, but there aren’t so many pieces that the assembler gets sick of the photo before finishing the puzzle.  One thousand pieces make the puzzle a lot more challenging and satisfying than an ordinary 500-piece tabletop puzzle, but the photo is an easy one that reduces the difficulty level.  As always with Ravensburger puzzles, this one is excellently made, with unique pieces that fit together in only one way.  The soft-click connections tell you immediately whether or not you have joined the correct pieces.

Kids who love playing “Minecraft” will love this LEGO MINECRAFT THE CREEPER MINE 21155 BUILDING KIT (834 Pieces).  It’s one of the biggest sets in the LEGO “Minecraft” series--the series that brings various locales in the virtual “Minecraft” universe to life.  The “Minecraft” sets can be joined together, and they use standard LEGO elements that are compatible with all regular LEGO sets.  A great feature is the twisty “mine shaft” inside the Creeper Mine tower.  You pour “gold nuggets” (gold-colored studs) into the top, open a switch, and the “nuggets” fall down an angled shaft into a treasure chest waiting below.  (It reminds me of a coin sorter.)  The set has Steve, blacksmith, and husk minifigs, plus buildable Creeper, cow, and bat figures.  There’s a mine car that runs on rails, and TNT that blasts out bedrock.  This set is a bit trickier to build than other “Minecraft” sets, but it’s a satisfying build.  If you have a mobile device that is compatible with the “LEGO Life” app, you can download the building instructions for this set and build it from your tablet (rather than from the included printed instructions).

This MOONLITE STORYBOOK PROJECTOR (Special Edition Disney Gift Pack, for Ages 1 and Up) is a flawed concept.  The projector is difficult to position over the flashlight on my Galaxy S10 phone, unless I turn the phone over and look through the lens at the light.  This burns an image into my retina, and is clearly not good for my eyes!  The projector clips onto the phone, and exerts moderate pressure on the screen.  I don’t like this, because I worry about cracking the screen.  The projector works with the free “Moonlite” app (available from the App Store or Google Play).  You will have to register for an account to use the app.  After that, you must activate each set of story reels by entering an activation code--the code is on a small card packed in the box.  You tap the “unlock” icon at the top left of the app, and enter the code.  Once you’ve activated the story reel set, you click on the story you want.  The app will then display a story for you to read to your child.  As you read each page, you advance the reel in the Moonlite projector, to get a new illustration for each page of the story.  The projector shows a focused illustration (focus is adjustable), as long as you are no more than a yard or so away from the surface you are projecting to.  You cannot project the picture across a room, or for any great distance, because the phone’s flashlight isn’t bright enough to project for long distances.  The photos that accompany the stories in the Disney set are boring, as are the stories.  Kids will no doubt love the Disney characters in the pictures (e.g., Mickey, Minnie, Donald, Goofy), but the story illustrations are repetitious.  Also the detail in the illustrations gets lost because the projected images are fairly small.

This LEGO CLASSIC CREATIVE BLUE BRICKS 11006 KIDS’ STARTER SET (New 2020 (52 Pieces)) is a very small building set, but it has an extremely well-chosen variety of LEGO elements. (In my photo, the whale’s transparent blue waterspout is mostly hidden under a black brick at the lower right.)  Printed step-by-step building instructions for the critters are included in the box.  Better yet, the set has enough varied elements to allow kids to rebuild the suggested models into small creatures or models of their own design.  Adults can use this small set as a relaxing “fiddle” set.  The all-blue set is a great way to introduce LEGO to someone new.  If the person enjoys playing with LEGO elements, then he/she can invest in larger sets later.  The set contains standard LEGO pieces that are compatible with all LEGO building sets except DUPLO.  (DUPLO sets are intended for very young children, and have extra-large bricks and pieces.)

Who doesn't love a shiny new box of Crayola crayons?  When I was little, my family could not afford anything bigger than the school-required box--I think at the time it was 16 crayons.  I recall looking longingly at the 49-count box, and the 64-count box was definitely out of the question.  So now, along comes this great box of 152 crayons, and I could not resist.  I am tempted to say that you will find every color known to man in this set of crayons, but I have been to a paint store recently and I know that is not true.  However, I feel safe in saying you will find every color you could ever want.  The classics?  Yes.  Glitter crayons?  Got 'em.  Neon colors?  Yup!  There is even a handy-dandy crayon sharpener in the center of the box.  The plastic cover is a bit flimsy, but that is my only critique.  Each summer, a group I belong to collects school supplies for needy children in our area.  We scrutinize "Back to School" supply lists, and I note that every single school specifies  a 24-count box of Crayola crayons.  The competing brands are just not acceptable, and I think the teachers are wise to insist on Crayola.  You just cannot go wrong.  Happy artistic endeavors with your new rainbow of color!

Giorgione's Watercolor Paint palette Set for Kids 48 Colors was presented well in a nice hinged tin. A brush, pencil, tube of white, packet of 8 sheets of watercolor paper and a refillable brush pen are included. To refill the brush pen, twist it open in the opposite direction than you may anticipate. I recommend using distilled water for watercolors, it's not expensive and is available in most grocery stores.  This is a 48 color set of full pans. The variety is acceptable, but there is no color chart, colors are not labeled at all. The issue with this set is the pigments are weak, and the binder is very heaving making them all very opaque and chalky when dry. They blend okay, and blooming leaves much to be desired. The tin states it is compliant to ASTM D4236 and EN71. There is no way to verify this information. For this reason, I would be hesitant to recommend it for use by children.  I do not find the set to be a good value. There are other budget sets available in the same price range that prove to me much easier to use, with better pigment saturation. As an adult fan of LEGO, I really love assembling the Creator and City sets with fancy buildings.  These usually come with minifigs, but sometimes the streets and buildings seem empty without more people.  The City “People Packs” solve this problem!  This LEGO “FUN AT THE BEACH” BUILDING KIT (60153) gives you a wide selection of minifigs attired in swimsuits, wetsuits, and playsuits, with appropriate accessories.  As always, it was fun to see how the brick-built accessories (sand castle, lounge chair, volleyball net, lifeguard tower, beach umbrella, ice cream stand, hammock) transformed from regular LEGO bricks into things that looked like something else.  This set has a lot of specialized accessory bricks (metal detector, sand pail, Dalmatian dog, porpoise, lobster, kayak with paddle, windsurfing board, banana, fish, shovel, volleyball, camera, binoculars, boombox, cellphone, bullhorn) that are nice to have.  The minifigs can be assembled per the instructions, or mixed and matched.  There are people of different ages (from grandmother to kids), and many different hairstyles (short, long and loose, braids, buns) in different colors (gray, brown, beige, yellow).  These beach-attired minifigs are just what you need if you're creating your own ocean- or beach-themed models from regular LEGO bricks.

This LEGO FRIENDS ANDREA'S HEART BOX  41354 BUILDING KIT (84 Pieces) makes a sweet little (3.5" wide x 3" high) treasure box that can also be used to store the Andrea figure and her accessories.  The accessories include a mic, a guitar, an ice cream cone, hair ribbons, a headphone set, a sound speaker, and a stand for displaying the heart box in an upright position.  The colorful box also doubles as a display stand for Andrea.  (The fuchsia square on the box lid has a stud that holds the little doll securely.)  When placed atop the box, surrounded by her accessories, Andrea is set up for a musical performance.  The heart-shaped box is quick and easy to assemble.  The lid fits snugly, and there's room for a charm bracelet or a few rings.  The building kit is one of a series that features each of the LEGO Friends characters.  The completed Friends treasure boxes also stack--a nice extra touch, as you would expect to see from LEGO.

This LEGO UNIKITTY CLOUD CAR BUILDING KIT (126 Piece) is a small kit that an adult can assemble in half an hour; the building techniques are simple and straightforward, so that small children shouldn’t find it difficult.  There are two elements that I particularly like, both on Unikitty’s cloud car--a propeller that spins with a flick of the finger, and a pair of spring-loaded “sparkle matter” guns that fire dots when you press the catch on the special LEGO gun element.  I also like the use of a blue crystal element that becomes a fountain in a tiny park.  The figures have their faces printed on special bricks (no stickers).  Unikitty has a wide, open-mouthed grin; her head turns, and she fits in a space in the cloud car.  Other figures are Square Bear and Feebee.  Princess Unikitty was introduced in “The LEGO Movie”.  At first I thought that a hybrid unicorn/cat was creepy, but now I’m totally charmed by the “Unikitty” TV series that airs on the Cartoon Channel, and by the LEGO Unikitty sets.  I only wish that the Unikitty minifigs with different versions of Unikitty (see [[ASIN:B07BMF3K48 Unikitty Add-On Item]] were marketed as a straightforward collection, instead of as expensive grab-bag “mystery figures”.

[[VIDEOID:19354301]]This gizmo is fun for about 6 minutes and 20 seconds, not including set-up time.  It's okay to a point once you're using the coloring pages or stencils supplied, but doesn't encourage self-expression or creativity.  Pros: 1. No mess, other than the occasional splatter of white stuff, which is easy to clean up. 2. Sprayer makes a professional sounding noise 3. Includes three stencils, with interesting patterns 4. Easy to set up 5. Includes everything you need except batteries  Cons: 1. It uses FOUR "C" batteries (not included) 2. The colors only work on Crayola special paper. 3. The colors are pale, washed out looking tints of the colors they claim to be 4. The colors don't show up immediately, and as a result, kids tend to spray the same area over and over again, causing a wet spot 5. The coloring pages are specially created so that the paint only sticks to specific areas, thereby guaranteeing a good finish.  Similarly, the stencils only let through the paint in the right places. Kids can draw their own images using the markers and crayon, but it's impossible to keep the spray within the lines, resulting in major frustration for kids who want to express themselves beyond the coloring pages and stencils.  Recommended only for short term entertainment, when you don't feel like cleaning up afterwards.  Not for creative kids.  Amanda Richards, March 20, 2008

There are some LEGO kits that really shine, and this LEGO STAR WARS RESISTANCE TRANSPORT POD BUILDING KIT (75176; 294 Pieces) is one of them.  It’s an interesting build, with some elements attached underneath (studs not on top), many added to the sides, and several specialized parts (e.g., canopy, bolt launchers).  The finished build is solid, and provides a toy that’s fun to play with (even for adults).  The pod has room inside for all three minifigs (Rose, Finn, BB8).  The pod canopy, rear compartment roof, and side storage compartment door lift off and go back on easily.  The cockpit has holders for Finn’s and Rose’s weapons.  The storage compartment has extra weapons.  The pod has a side turret gun that swivels for aiming when you turn the big knob at the back.  The turret gun has a trigger that launches single rounds.  The pod shoots long red bolts (rounded tips, not pointed) from underneath the cockpit. (Two special-to-the-kit bricks have springs inside that work as launchers).  The pod feels good in your hand.  The kit takes two hours or less to build; it uses some Technic connectors in addition to standard bricks and plates.  The instructions are crystal clear.  There are quite a few stickers to add to the bricks, but the graphics are nice when all the stickers are in place.  This is a great small LEGO kit, one that I’ll be keeping for display and play.

Elefun is a simple game that requires no special setup and no special strategy. It is a somewhat physical game involving the catching of butterflies in a small net. These vinyl butterflies are placed in the base, the trunk is attached, the motor is turned on, and the butterflies slowly release from Elefun. The person who catches the most is the winner. It is a game that can be learned instantly and can be used to provide some quick entertainment when children are getting restless.  Elefun offers some good points that make it a hit with most children; namely the action factor, which encourages children to physically compete as they scramble with their nets in hand, trying to catch as many butterflies as possible to win the game. But other than this, Elefun isn't as much fun as its name suggests. First, the action factor is actually a little on the slow side. I can agree that a gradual release of the butterflies from Elefun's trunk is a good idea, but the problem is the amount of time it takes for the trunk to expand and the slowness of the release of the vinyl butterflies. The motor is too weak to get the game going and some children will grow impatient having to wait.  Another problem with Elefun is its tendency to eat up batteries. The game takes four batteries and they die quickly, mainly because the game is very brief and most children will want to play it again and again. If this happens, the batteries are not likely to last more than a few days. It is a little puzzling that four batteries would produce so little power and also die out so quickly, but this is exactly what happens. Then, there is the problem with the butterflies themselves. There are thirty of them, but no matter how they are placed in the base, Elefun doesn't release them all. There are always a few butterflies left, stuck in the base. Their small size makes them easy to lose, too.  Kids like action- oriented games and most children will like Elefun at first. But once they are forced to stand around, waiting for the butterflies to release and once they realize how quickly the batteries fail, they will grow frustrated with this game and push it aside in favor of more reliable entertainment. It could be improved with a few simple changes, but until that happens, don't waste your time or money on Elefun.

The LEGO STAR WARS SANDCRAWLER BUILDING KIT (1239 Piece) is a kit for serious “Star Wars” fans, because it’s basically a Tatooine garbage/trash truck.  There’s not a whole lot of action surrounding a Jawa sandcrawler, but the LEGO model makes up for this by including many hinged and sliding panels that open up the interior to show Jawas and their salvaged droids and space junk inside.  This set doesn’t have a huge number of pieces (1239), and the finished model isn’t particularly large (12” long, 7” high, 4” wide).  However, like all LEGO sets, it’s interesting and fun to build.  My favorite part is the chains on the four tractor wheels--the elements are sort of fiddly to connect up, but the finished tractor chains are convincingly realistic.  There are some Technic elements included to make the tractor wheels work, and to make it possible to steer the sandcrawler with an external gear wheel at the front.  Minifigs included are young Luke Skywalker, 2 Jawas, an RA-7 droid, an R5-A2 astromech droid, and a medical droid.  If you want some shooting action, this set pairs nicely with the [[ASIN:B075SCRBQ6 Tatooine Battle Pack]].

I was a little surprised with some of the negative reviews. If you don't regularly buy rocks and minerals, I can see that you might be disappointed, but my 23 year old son buys and collects them and they are often very small pieces. If you want larger ones, it's going to cost a pretty penny! Now, until I opened it, I was a little worried. There was a lot of noise when I shook it. I was afraid everything would be broken and crumbly or that items had gotten mixed up inside.  I was very pleasantly surprised. Everything was in tact and looked great. I got this calendar for my son that I mentioned above. I always get my kids a fun advent countdown calender. I was able to open it from the top and slide the tray out without damaging the perforated doors/windows (or whatever you call the openings). The tray was divided and everything was in its place and looked great. I do not know a lot about rocks and minerals, but from my novice point of view, they are nice. Some are raw, others are polished. There are fossils in addition to the rocks and minerals. The first thing you open is a little magnifying glass and cloth. My son uses a jewelry loupe to examine his collection, but for a new collector, I'm sure what is provided will be useful. The quality of the items seems to be good and comparable to some of the ones that I have purchased for my son in the past.  In order to get the large, cool ones, you have to spend a lot of money! This is actually impressive to receive such a variety of items at this cost. I think my son will have fun opening it.

What kid doesn't like dinosaurs?  This is perfert for your boy or girl who is into dinosaurs.  I suppose it could be considered an educational toy, but probably only marginally so.  Who says educational toys can't be fun too?  This is especially nice for a kid interested in archeology.  Contains a block of plaster that has embedded into it "DNA" stickers and a glow in the dark dinosaur.  After the fun of digging out the dinosaur and stickers with the included digging tools and brush, they are left with a dinosaur model, so even though the digging fun is over after one time (if they do it all at once), there is something to show for it.  There are also two "augmented reality" (AR) play mats that are completed with the DNA stickers that have been "excavated".  The mats when used with a cell phone and an application from the App Store (for Apple phones) or the Google Play Store (for Androids) produces a 3D dinosaur, visible on the phone.  One problem with this is that it might not work with all cell phones.  It is supposed to work with MOST Apple devices (8.0 and above).  On Android phones the only devices tested are Google Nexus Series, Samsung Galaxy S5 and Samsung Galaxy Tab.  I didn't have a cell phone to test this on, but it seems like it still might be fun even if that part doesn't work.  It's just that you might not be able to use the full capability of the kit if your phone doesn't work with the application.  Also if you give this to a child of only 8 (the lower age limit on this), they may not yet have a cell and you may not want them to have one at that age.  Since this contains many small parts, it needs to be kept away from small children, as there is a choking hazard (box says no one under 3).  I would have loved a toy like this when I was a kid.  Just remember to put down a newspaper when they are working on the plaster block in order to collect all the plaster pieces that are chipped off as they excavate.  Pricing of the kit is very reasonable.  Made in China.

This game is electronic and may be more enticing for children compared to the regular Yahtzee.  It is also fairly portable which makes it convenient to take with you on trips.  However, this game is more expensive than the regular game and is not, in my opinion, worth the extra money. This is a fun-sounding game, and I like both the gameplay and concept. It feels like a mashup of dominoes and scrabble, only with colors, too. ***HOWEVER*** if you are red-green color blind, don't bother. The colors on some of the tiles look very similar to those with this condition: blue and purple look identical to me, for example. This made playing the game much more difficult for me than it was for everyone else. In addition to the need to think spatially, and in terms of patterns and combinations of symbols, I also had the added challenge of not being able to tell which color was which--I literally had to ask for help figuring out the colors every single time. This was extremely frustrating and completely ruined the game for me. It was like torture. Not enjoyable or fun. I can see it being a lot of fun for those without color blindness, but for me, it was completely unplayable. I have attached a picture of the game tiles--if the top two rows of tiles look the same to you (as they do for me), you won't be able to play this game. I understand they make a version for the color-blind--awesome. Go find that and buy it instead.

This is a  fine art set for kids, though I do have a couple of concerns.  First, the positive:  The set is well packaged.  The pencils are vibrant on the page, unlike some less expensive colored pencils.  The pastels are nice, with an even greater variety of colors.  The set includes drawing paper and sandpaper.  However, there are just a couple of minor negatives:  The set is targeted at really young children, and I do not think most of them would have the small motor skills to manipulate such slim pencils and narrow pastels.  The right age is probably about eight.  I also think the set is somewhat overpriced.  You could get each of these items separately (and much more drawing paper) for less money.  That said, this is a nice set for a budding artist.  It would make a good gift, especially if you like all the materials in well packaged in one place.

This LEGO SYSTAR PARTY CREW BUILDING KIT (from THE LEGO MOVIE 2; Set 70848, New 2019, 196 Pieces) is kinda fiddly to assemble.  It has a lot of pieces, but they’re mostly small pieces (1 or 2 studs).  Mixed in are some specialty pieces (e.g., Unikitty’s cloud stole, the golden scallop shell, the piano keyboards) that require considerable creativity to be used in other projects.  As an adult Unikitty fan (don’t ask me why!), I love the new Unikitty figure.  She has fancy boots, and two different faces.  One face has sunglasses, the other has a starry-eyed expression.  Unikitty’s cloud stole adds a touch of elegance.  The finished pirate figure, with all of its one-man-band instruments, stands up solidly.  This is a tribute to the designer, because the arms and back have a lot of weight to support.  The arms and legs can be repositioned in their ball-socket joints.  If you download the Lego Life app (available for both iOS and Android), you can scan the maze code on the front of the printed instructions and get building instructions for an “Intergalactic Drum Set” (see photo).  This uses parts from the basic build, and extends the building fun.  Scanning the maze code also provides the set’s building instructions, in case you prefer to build from a tablet or cellphone display.

For several years, we have done a game night with two other couples at least once a month - we play cards, dominos, board games, murder mystery dinners, laser tag, basketball on horses and we've even done the Escape Room thing. Playing this game tonight was a natural way to test it out. The Horrible Truth is that this one will not ever be in our rotation again.  The box is very clearly marked "Explicit Content", so the inclusion of profanity, sexual innuendo, and bodily function jokes wasn't a surprise. We're a pretty open minded and easy going group and it takes more than a few tacky questions or obscene stick figures to bother any of us. There are a lot of those things in the game, BTW.  Our issues were related to the structure of the game and it's execution. The instructions and actual game play are overly complicated for no obvious reason. The voting and answer tiles are too small for big fingers to grip easily and it really needs a little holder for them - like for Scrabble tiles. The game itself is trying too hard to be too many things - a bit of Truth or Dare mixed with Explicit Pictionary and a little Cards Against Humanity. We found the Draw It cards elicited the most laughs and whole group engagement. My final complaint is the misspelled words on several cards! Seriously? This is just bad editing and bad quality control and I certainly wouldn't pay money for a game with typos.

This is a pretty good case. And, I'll get to the 'pros' or the good stuff later. But, first, let's talk about the 'cons'. Even though the lining foam is very well cut the the shape of the drone, and has plenty of room for batteries and other accessories, it still has a few drawbacks. One, the foam is so stiff that it really won't absorb much of the impact. This is the primary reason for using foam instead of, let's say, rigid plastic. It's still better than just letting the drone bang around in an empty case. But, they could have done better.  Next, they COULD have included a pocket SOMEWHERE to contain booklets, instructions, small parts etc. This case is actually big enough that they could have included a hinged divider with the eggcrate packing on one side and more storage space on the back. As it is, you just have to dump all your small stuff in one of the square spaces in the case and hope they don't fall out of that space and get scattered throughout the case.  Finally, and this is my main gripe... The case is designed to accommodate a proprietary shoulder strap (made exactly to fit this case).  However, the seller has found it unnecessary to include that shoulder strap or even offer it as an option. In fact, with as many questions as were asked about the slots on the case and a shoulder strap, the seller has yet to offer an intelligent reply. So sad.  Even though I am going to keep this case and use it, it's only because I don't do a lot of traveling and this case will mainly be used for storage. That, and I just don't want to go through the hassle of finding another case.  Now, for the pros:  The case is large enough to hold the quad, with a battery installed, three more batts, the controller (standard or pro), multi-charger plate, lens filter set and all of the other peripherals, plus any other accessories that would be appropriate for this drone (except the landing pad).  The case is very ruggedly designed and built. It's super sturdy and I could probably jump up and down on it without damaging the drone inside. (But, because nothing fits super snug and the foam is very stiff, slamming it around like would be done in most baggage handling scenarios may cause some damage.)  The new design of the closures make them more like modern luggage and less like a pelican case. The operation of these closures is much simpler and easier.  There is a one way valve that will equalize the air pressure in the case to high altitudes, but prevent air from re-entering at lower altitudes. This is good to control the amount of humidity that gets in.  The neoprene gasket that seals the case is very well done. Even the seam closing the loop is done well. Thus, improving the air tight seal of the case.  The folding handle is super rigid, is NOT coming loose or off and has the hard rubber grip that makes it a little easier to handle.  It seems that the maker of the case tried to deal with the basics and did an 'okay' job. But, not great. Then, the seller has taken that product and made it less attractive by leaving out a very pertinent component.  So, yes. I'm keeping it. Just to store my new Mavic Air 2s in. Not to transport. If I really were traveling a lot, and taking my Air 2s with me, I would not have chosen this case.  2 stars.  Update: I am returning it. It's too expensive and bulky to use for storage alone.

This claims to be "retro/classic" games, and they all sort of are reminiscent of games I remember growing up... it feels like an emulator playing the classics (like I have on my phone) and they even make it look like the original Gameboy, but they're not the games I remember. They're cheap knock-offs. I'm guessing this company wanted to sell this device as an emulator for the classics but couldn't get the rights to the original games, so they modified them enough to be different games loosely based on the originals. For example, games like "plumbers" are clearly supposed to be a parody of Mario Bros, but none of them are the Mario Bros I remember and wanted to relive. I feel tricked. Also, a lot of the games aren't unique games but merely alternative versions, sometimes they feel like later levels of the same game, and many more aren't even really so interesting to play. The 500+ number is misleading. The UI is also cluttered as there's no way to sort the games and they don't appear to always be in alphabetical order, so you sort of have to scroll until you find the one you want which is annoying. You also can't remove the ones you don't want to clean it up either. Honestly, I really REALLY wish it were just an emulator and you could use a USB cable or something to put your own ROM files like I have on my phone of the original games. And maybe even the ability to remove the ones I'm not interested in so the interface is cleaner. I like the physical layout of this with its physical buttons more than my phone, the second player controller is a treat, as well as the ability to plug this into a big screen (hello minivan entertainment system!), but these just aren't the games I want to play. I want the real retro classics. Not knock-offs. I feel tricked. This could be better if they gave us more control.

My 6 year old niece has been struggling with math. On-line learning during the pandemic has been challenging. As a former teacher, I have been trying to find ways to help her. If she was at school in-person, she would have access to base ten blocks in her classroom. At home, she is limited to 2-D illustrations. This GLISTELLO 1000-PIECE JIGSAW PUZZLE FOR ADULTS - BURANO, ITALY - VENICE (27.5 x 19.7 inches) is a good quality puzzle, but one for dedicated experts.  It has many solid-colored pieces, and lots of blue pieces (sky and water) that are almost exactly the same shade of blue.  These kinds of pieces must be assembled by shape, which greatly increases the difficulty of the puzzle.  Even the border of this puzzle is difficult to assemble, because of all the similar-colored blue pieces.  The puzzle pieces are small, which makes the 1000-piece puzzle fit easily on a worktable.  The fully interlocking pieces are thick enough to be handled easily, and the interlocking tabs don’t bend if the pieces are handled with ordinary care.  However, the small size of the pieces makes them harder to pick up during the pre-assembly sorting process.  The pieces don’t click together snugly like Ravensburger jigsaw pieces, but they fit together unmistakably by feel when properly joined.  Unfortunately, the pieces don’t fit tightly enough to allow joined sections to be picked up and moved without falling apart.  This is mildly annoying, because I like to turn questionably joined pieces over to check the fit from the wrong side--especially when the pieces are a solid color that requires assembly by shape.  The puzzle picture looks exactly like the photograph on the product page.  There are large areas of bright color, and not much fine detail.  The picture is nicely printed on the puzzle itself.  The price (about $15 on Amazon) is very reasonable.  I like this puzzle, because I like to assemble extra-difficult jigsaws.  However, I wouldn’t recommend it for group assembly, because of the many pieces that are visually quite similar in color and shape.  Puzzles like this one require a lot of sorting of pieces in advance by color and shape if the actual assembly is to proceed fairly quickly.

My kids adore Lalaloopsy! They were thrilled to get this treehouse. It is cute and fun.  The pieces are tiny. Not just the dolls, which are much smaller than I would have anticipated, but there are little accessories that are so tiny. It'd be difficult for an adult to play with these tiny accessories. The treehouse itself is a nightmare. If you manage to get it out of its packaging without breaking it, you then get to refer to a diagram to try to set the thing up. Tiny ropes to make tiny zip lines. Tiny swings with tiny attachments. It took me a good 15 minutes to get everything set up like it showed in the diagram. Then one of the kids touches it. Then they beg me to reset up what they just messed up. And on and on it goes. Me resetting the thing up every 5 minutes, because they certainly can't do it. After a day or so, they forgot the zipline was there, forgot about the pulleys, overlooked the swings, and just played with the dolls around the treehouse, not in the treehouse.  This is a very frustrating toy. It is too small, too fragile, and too complicated for kids to actually enjoy. I can't see any kid under the age of 12 begin able to appropriately work this toy. Fortunately, Hot Wheels cars are fairly cheap, but I cannot understand why the bigger, more complicated Hot Wheels sets are always released with only one car.  The set requires two D-cell batteries, which are NOT included.  This kit is NOT a good choice for Hot Wheels newbies, even though it’s attractive and fun.  You get the most bang for your buck with the starter kits like the [[ASIN:B01JMYP9OQ Hot Wheels Track Builder Stunt Box]]. Late last year, for a week or so, all 3 of our cats sat gathered around the stove in the kitchen just staring at it. I quickly figured that was probably NOT a good sign-that we likely had an uninvited guest in our home. A few days later, I was proven correct.  Got up early one morning to take the dog out and there was  a dead mouse by the front door waiting to greet me, with 2 of the 3 cats sitting close by, proud of what they had left for me. (I don't think I've ever screamed louder in my entire life than I did that morning! lolol-I did heap praise on them after I calmed down)  Unfortunately, the cats were once again gathered around the stove watching...and a few days later, mouse #2  was waiting for us to clean up. (Thank God I didn't find that one)  As soon as I saw this cat toy, I immediately ordered it.  Though one of our 3 cats who helped catch the mice has since gone to the Rainbow Bridge, I knew the other 2, Miss Piggy and Squeaky, would have loads of fun playing with this toy. (And if I'm being honest, I want to keep them in tip-top shape in case any further 'uninvited guests' find their way into our home! lol) The mouse (I see it more of a rat than a mouse) is nearly 11 inches long from the tip of the nose to the end of the tail.  It has beady red eyes that light up.  From a distance, I believe people (who don't know its fake) could mistaken it for a real mouse (rat).  It has gray fur and what appears to be sharp teeth sticking out of its mouth. (The teeth are NOT sharp, they only appear that way) The mouse takes 3 AAA batteries (not included) and the remote controller takes 2 AAA batteries as well. (not included)  The only assembly required is placing the batteries in the mouse and controller. A nice, small screwdriver comes with the toy, something I especially appreciate! (Works on my eyeglass hinges as well!) The controller allows you to move the mouse forward and backward. (Both of our young kids were able to operate the remote control without issue while we stood with them) It does work on carpet but works far better on flooring.  As soon as we turned it on and began steering it around the room, 2 cats immediately popped out of their beds, their curiosity piqued.  Both cats watched the mouse zip around the floor for a bit.  As soon as it got close to Miss Piggy, she jumped and ran under the bed. lol.  But Squeaky...he was far braver than Piggy and watched it up close.  We'd stop it for awhile and he'd creep closer and closer to it, staring at it....and then we'd start it up again. lol.  It was loads of fun to watch him with this toy.  It certainly kept him entertained for quite some time! Piggy stayed hidden but she's a nosy cat...she'll get the hang of it before long.  This is a fun toy not only for cats but for cat owners to operate for their cats! We had loads of fun watching the cat's reaction.  Its a great way for your cat to get some exercise as well.  Very easy to operate also!  UPDATE******************  We got this out again tonight to play with. This time, the dog had more fun chasing it than the cats. However, after just under 15 minutes of use, it stopped working entirely!  Even though I had used brand new batteries in the mouse and the controller when I first got it (a day ago), I replaced all 5 batteries.  It does NOT work at all.  Nothing happened to it. It did not fall, it didn't' crash, it was not damaged in any way, it simply stopped working altogether in the blink of an eye. And that's a darn shame because my cat AND dog had fun with it. Due to the fact that it stopped working entirely after just under 30 minutes of total use since we got it, and the fact we did nothing to break it/make it stop working, I've changed my rating/review of this item.  I cannot recommend an item that stops working out of the blue as this did.  For the minutes that it did work, my pets loved it.  And we loved watching them have fun.

This WOWWEE STEALTH (GREEN) UNTAMED RAPTOR (Collectible Dinosaur by Fingerlings-Interactive) is just the cutest toy!  It’s extremely hard to put down, even for an adult like myself.  It roars when poked, it purrs when stroked, it blinks its eyes, it goes to sleep in your hand.  It even delivers “dino gas” when you press and hold the nose and back of the head.  Mine works perfectly out of the box, and is mesmerizing.  With a little practice, I was able to get consistent responses from my little guy (e.g., tapping 1, 2, or 3 times on the nose would consistently produce the same sounds and behavior, depending on the number of taps).  The easiest actions are the transition from untamed to tamed (stroke from nose to back of head) and tamed to untamed (press and hold back of head).  I can well believe that a child would be very, very disappointed if the raptor suddenly stopped working, so parents should be prepared for this possibility.  If yours should stop working, even if the batteries are good, be sure to contact the manufacturer--the web address is in the instructions.
