Our players were my husband and I, along with our sixteen year old daughter and 19 year old son. The game itself is pretty creative. The instructions were okay, but there were a couple of times when clarification would have been helpful. For example, the first couple of cards we played, one of the teammates read the card. Then we had one card come up that it didn't make sense for anyone on that team to see the card in advance, so we started having the opponents read the cards. The questions and activities were fun. My daughter enjoyed the play-doh activities. I wish it came with a small laminated type card or mat to use with the play-doh. I didn't want to use it directly on our dining room table, so the first time it came up, we used it on a piece of paper. A lot of it stuck to the paper and we couldn't get it off. There are some multiple choice questions, true and false statements, drawing, sculpting, humming, & acting involved. I had to hum Bohemian Rhapsody to my daughter! The game was fun, but a lot more difficult than we expected. Both teams were stuck on the board for a while because of incorrect answers. I think this would be better suited to larger groups or a party setting. Overall, we liked it, but didn't love it.

I love LEGO building kits, but the LEGO Star Wars figures are NOT my favorites.  I prefer the regular Technic and Creator kits, which have fewer specialty parts that can't be used for anything else.  This LEGO STAR WARS “ELITE PRAETORIAN GUARD” BUILDING KIT (75529; 92 Pieces) takes about half an hour to assemble.  It produces a posable figure with a fascinating double-knife-tipped weapon that moves from side to side when you move the figure’s shoulders (from the back of the figure).  However, new poses must be done with care, because the ball joints are stiff and the armor is clipped vertically onto ball connectors.  There's no good place to hold the body when you reposition the arms and legs, so it's easy to knock pieces off, or to pull the figure apart.  The knife weapon must be taken apart at one hand to reposition it.  The kit uses various Technic connectors, so it helps to be familiar with these when you assemble the figure.  (The kit is slightly harder to assemble than a kit with traditional bricks.)  As always with LEGO kits, it's interesting to see how the specialty parts all combine into a final build that looks very different from the individual pieces.

This LEGO MARVEL SPIDER-MAN FAR FROM HOME MOLTEN MAN BATTLE 76128 BUILDING KIT (294 Pieces) is probably most appreciated by kids and adults who’ve watched the “Spider-Man Far From Home” movie that features Molten Man. The kit is compatible with standard LEGO sets, and has some elements that can be used very creatively in other models.  For example, the two gold chains that hang from the figure’s shoulders are unusual for the color.  Another element that I really love is the fast-action stud shooter hand.  (It shoots 6 studs in rapid succession as you turn the rotary gear part.)  The gripper claw hand is also pretty cool.  The minifigs and their accessories are especially good.  You get Spidey with an assortment of web weapons; a fireman with a buildable fire extinguisher; and a purple-caped Mysterio.  There are a lot of small elements in this set--which is why the piece count is so high.  If you have a mobile device that is compatible with the LEGO Life app, you can download the building instructions and build the model using your mobile device rather than the printed instructions.

This UBTECH JIMU ROBOT TANKBOT (Robot TankBot App-Enabled STEM Learning Kit (190 pcs)) makes a wonderful gift, because it’s so beautifully packaged.  It comes in a heavy-duty, magnetic-closure box that opens like a book.  Inside, the main controller, connectors and fasteners are packed in colorful paper boxes that nest perfectly in the big outer box.  The unboxing experience is beautifully presented--it reminds me of unboxing an Apple product.  The Tankbot is a very cute, sturdy little robot.  He’s fast and responsive, and moves extremely well on his plastic tank treads.  He’s controlled with an app (iOS or Android), using Bluetooth.  For me, pairing was almost instantaneous (once Tankbot was plugged into a power source).  He does NOT have a separate controller, so you must have a tablet or a smartphone to make him work.  (I use an iPad.)  You can’t even build him without a tablet or smartphone--you have to download the app and the building instructions.  This is a pet peeve of mine; I believe that the instructions for building toys belong in the box.  However, Ubtech should perhaps be forgiven, because the building instructions are written in something that resembles Autocad.  If you’re unsure about a connection in a particular step, you can rotate the model 360 degrees and look at it from every angle until you’re sure that you’ve got it right.  That said, Tankbot is something of a bear to build--much harder than the most difficult motorized LEGO sets.  There are three reasons for this: (1) the instructions don’t always show the elements recognizably or exactly the way they go together (e.g., the wires have a colored band that is on the wrong side in many drawings); (2) the instructions don’t always start at the easiest place for the next step in the assembly; and (3) the components click together so firmly, you need pliers to separate some of them if you make a mistake.  But the struggle is probably worthwhile, because you end up with a solid, programmable robot that you can drive around, use to pick up things, and spin in celebration (just like “Battlebots”).  Tankbot does take some practice at driving--I’m still learning the controls, but I’m not great at handling remote control toys in the first place.  I haven’t tried the programming portion of the app yet--even the simple programming is something you have to learn--but I have no reason to think it won’t work.  Note that Tankbot’s battery needs charging before you can do anything with him (including connecting to the controller app via Bluetooth).  There is a charger with an AC adapter included, and Tankbot charges fairly fast.  He does a firmware update automatically when you first connect him, if an update is available.

As an adult, I love this LEGO DUPLO TOWN TROPICAL ISLAND 10906 BUILDING SET (73 Pieces) every bit as much as a toddler (age 2+ and up) surely will.  The charming DUPLO figures (more doll-like than regular LEGO minifigures) include a caretaker, a traveler, a child, an elephant, a baby elephant, a tiger, a baby tiger, a monkey, and a toucan.  The play scene includes a treehouse with a pulley-operated basket for carrying up supplies, a caretaker’s house with a door that opens, a moveable “rope” bridge, and a vine that the monkey can swing on.  The set’s accessories include a camera, a bunch of bananas, a pineapple, and jungle vegetation.  The figures can be played with separately.  All of the figures and accessories can be placed in different locations around the scene.  My favorites are the toucan and the pineapple, but all of the figures and accessories are great.  Overall, this particular set is a delight, no matter what your age.  Note that the DUPLO sets, which are designed for very young builders with developing motor skills, are NOT compatible with standard LEGO sets.  The building blocks in DUPLO sets are much larger than regular LEGO bricks.  The 10906 Tropical Island set is currently unavailable on Amazon, but is still available in the online LEGO Store, as of June 2020.

These products, both the dry float lounger and the socializer are prime examples of why I test products the way I do prior to writing my review.  When I initially received these products and inflated them I was fairly impressed with the design and comfort - by 18 days later both were flat lumps of rubber in the trash.  First the socializer never held air.  After inflation by the following morning it had deflated to around 70% capacity. I put it under water in the pool and discovered multiple small defects in 2 seams.  The dry float lounger lasted a bit longer at 18 days but then was found flat due to a separation at the seam.  It is important to note neither of these were ever used by children or in any rough play as when I review products I adhere to strict guidelines - and there are no children in the home.  While the socializer is listed as a dry float it appears the user’s calves and feet would be in the water.  The lounger pool float is perfect for those who prefer to float completely out of the water. When floating on this no part of the body is submerged in the water.  While 88-90% of the world’s population is right handed this manufacturer chose to place the cup holder on the left side and made it too small to hold a standard 12 Oz can for both of these floats.  This is simply poor designing.  While these floats come with a small patch kit, these are only helpful when repairing a puncture and absolutely will not hold a torn seam.  While these floats appeared to have promise initially, in well under a month they proved to be poorly designed, and of substandard construction and materials.  I would not recommend either of these products.

BUDDY N BUDDIES Magic Shoes Toy doesn't provide much for play value. Several pieces are needed to make a total of 5 right shoes. Not pairs, just the right... which isn't a lot of fun after they are assembled. They don't fit any sort of popular doll, nor would they fit children. Once they are assembled, there's nothing to do with them other than take them apart and re-assemble. Some creativity is involved, and many, many small parts that are difficult for small hands to remove. It also makes some sharp points, so I don't feel comfortable recommending this toy to children. The sharp points left from removing the decorations to place in the pegs are dangerous, the very small parts can be lost easily, and may pose a choking hazard to small children or pets. It is recommend for children 3 and up, as it states on the package, but they are so small they can be easily lost. I find this shoe playset to be a poor value because once they are assembled, there is no re-play value. Very few children would find this appealing and it was a disappointing toy in my household. If the shoes came in pairs, fit popular dolls and the smaller pieces removed without sharp edges, this would be a good idea. As it is, it's just not a good value for the money, or time. When I ordered this K'NEX kit to review, I had in mind my nephew, who is thirteen, and his younger cousin who is six.  My idea was that they could play together with this building kit on a rainy day and perhaps learn something in the process, the older cousin helping the younger.  Well, the kit is a bit young for the 13-year-old, and a bit old for the six-year-old, but I am not at all sorry I got this toy.  The boys really enjoyed putting together two of the five possible toys, and decided to save the other three possibilities for another day.  The set itself is well packaged, with lots of small parts, and so not appropriate for very young children.  The kids in our family love LEGO toys, and they liked this kit almost as much.  I liked it for its educational value as well as its fun aspect.  I also like the physics aspect; they boys are learning physics and engineering principles without even being aware of it.  I would definitely buy more K'NEX products.  Highly recommended.

The Darth Vader TIE Fighter isn't exactly an aerodynamic shape, but Air Hogs managed to stay true to its form while making it reasonably flyable.  Compared to the underpowered RC X-Wing, this one has plenty of thrust provided by two tandem counter-rotating motors within the centerline duct.  You can maintain altitude with about 60% thrust.  After a few familiarization flights (and crashes), I was up to 100 feet flying wide circles searching for Rebel ships.  Level flight will requires a rather significant angle-of-attack, and with that pitch up attitude makes it look like it's being pulled along by an invisible string.  The compromise made in this design is a lot of wobble left and right (roll).  The thin rudder is just sufficient to provide controllable flight, though at times it looks more like a bat than a TIE fighter with the wobble  The trim knob does let you adjust the rudder's center position, but the right stick is not proportional, it's 3-position full left rudder, center rudder, and right rudder.  But all-in-all, this is true-to-form TIE Fighter that is flyable and fun out of the box.

Kids love to color, so a toy like the Crayola Crayon Maker would seem like an ideal addition to any home with children under the age of eight. Coloring with your child's own crayons he/she just made would seem like a great creative addition to an already fun activity.  Unfortunately, the fun with this toy stops at the concept itself. Making crayons is a great idea, but this device has several issues that prevent it from living up to its potential. The way it works is simple enough- you place the crayon material on a tray and set a timer. The heat from the lightbulb melts the crayons, which are then poured into a mold. You then wait for them to cool and they are ready to use. This sounds simple, but there are all sorts of problems along the way, and many of them seem to stem from Crayola's desire to apparently make the crayon maker as safe as possible.  One issue is with the locking mechanism. As you move from one step to the next, the device locks itself and you cannot unlock until the cycle is completed. For example, when the crayons are transferred to the molds, there is no way to unlock at this point. You are forced to wait for the crayons to cool before you can open. Issues with the timer are similarly frustrating- if your child accidentally sets the timer prematurely, you have no choice but to wait until it is finished. You cannot move the timer forward. You have to wait for it to complete its cycle.  Another issue I have is with the melting phase. There is no buzzer or anything to tell you that the crayons are ready to transfer to the molds. Thus, you have to pay close attention and make sure you are there, when the light bulb goes off, ready to transfer. If you don't act promptly, the crayons will cool off and solidify in the tray.  These frustrations might be tolerable enough for more patient types, but there is one ultimate frustration is enough to make you want to give up and ask for a refund. I am referring to the amount of crayons included in each package. You get only enough to make two crayons! You heard me correctly- two small crayons! When you consider what you have to go through just to get a pair of crayons and when you consider how inexpensive crayons are, it makes you wonder why you even bothered to obtain this device in the first place.  Making crayons can be fun and there are many ways to make them using ordinary household heat sources, utensils, etc. I recommend making crayons the old fashioned way and skipping the Crayola Crayon Maker completely. It is a good idea gone bad and I can only hope that Crayola has a new and improved version in the making.

This Lincoln Logs Centennial set brings back a lot of memories. I remember playing with my mom's old set of Lincoln Logs when I was a child. I wasn't sure what kind of changes or improvements could be made to this classic toy, but the improvements that I've noticed are really for the better. The actual logs are solid and compressed. I remember my mom's old set was chipped in a few places, particularly the notches. These don't look like they will chip at all. The old ones were flat, matte whereas these are sealed and slightly glossy for added protection. I expected the plastic to be light weight and cheap, but it's heavy duty and very comparable to what I remember as a child. The shingles are plastic now instead of wood and so are the animals and figures. There is a nice assortment of pieces. The instruction booklet gives you step by step details on how to build your own cabin as well as pictures of other finished ones. I love that it comes in a metal tin. My mom's old set was a cardboard container that didn't hold up well over the years. It says that this is for ages 3 and up, but I plan to give this to my 6 year old nephew for Christmas. I think he'll get more out of the actual building and construction than a younger child would.

This MANHATTAN TOY STACK AND PULL TRAIN is irresistibly cute!  It's ready to play with, right out of the box--the three wooden train cars are already assembled, and the wooden stacking blocks are loose so you can immediately begin fitting them onto the poles that "attach" them to the cars.  The toy is well-made, all-wooden, and rolls smoothly across my living room carpet with the lightest of tugs on the woven pull cord.  There are only a few ways to fit all of the stacking blocks squarely onto the train cars--the blocks with curved sides (that fit with the cylinder-shaped blocks) align with the diagonally cut blocks, but their plain sides don't sit flat on the cars.  I found myself looking at the picture on the box, to get all the blocks loaded onto the cars in the best possible arrangement--and had to laugh at myself!  No small child would be the least bit bothered by stacking his or her blocks in a way that leaves spaces between the blocks.  I love the bright colors of these blocks, and the cheerful expressions of the farmer, the cow, and the pig.  The colors harmonize no matter how the blocks are arranged, and the painted triangles, dots, and curlicues add extra variety to the possible building-block combinations.  A detail that I particularly like, is the pair of yellow circles for headlights that appear on an edge of the green block that is used to represent the train or tractor engine.  This is an extremely well-thought-out toy.  Also, this is a marvelously decorative toy.  I think it will look great on a shelf in a child's room, long after the child has outgrown playing with it. Best of all, it's a toy that makes me smile when I look at it.

This LEGO FRIENDS MIA’S PLAY CUBE  (41403 Building Kit, New 2020 (40 Pieces)) is fun for any kid who loves doll houses and/or miniatures.  When you open the assembled cube, there’s a little world inside:  Mia dressed as a veterinary, ready to vaccinate her little rabbit “patient”.  The vet’s office is decorated with three stickers:  a poster in the back of the right half, and a meter and a display screen with a rabbit pic  in the left half.  (There are bonus stickers that can be used anywhere a child likes--perhaps on the outside of the cube?)  The assemblies stay solidly in place when the cube halves are closed for travel.  Something that I love about this set, is that the “surprise” animal for this set 41403 is always a rabbit, but a rabbit in one of four different colors.  I was happy to get a gold rabbit, but was relieved that I felt no pressure to “collect them all” when it came to the “surprise”.  The current play cube series sets have animal surprises as follows:  birds (41400); puppies (41401); mice (41402); rabbits (this set 41403); and cats ((41404).  The furnishings in Mia’s cube are easy to build, though you need to study the drawings carefully because there is NOT a separate step for each piece that is added.  When you’re finished, you will probably have some extra single pieces left over (e.g., a few dots, an extra syringe).  However, if you have any two-stud pieces left, you’ ve made a mistake and have left something out.

As an adult fan of LEGO, I really love assembling the City sets.  The beauty of these sets is the careful attention to detail.  In this LEGO CITY “BUS STATION” BUILDING KIT (60154), for example, the minifig passengers include a person in a wheelchair, and two people with bicycles.  The bicycles even clip onto a “rack” on the back of the big bus, or fit into a “locking station” alongside the bus shelter.  The set also includes a newsstand (complete with seller and printed newspaper tiles), a bus driver, and an office worker.  Stickers add bus schedules and advertisements to the shelter, and handicapped access decals for the bus doors.  With 337 regular LEGO pieces, this set provides a satisfying amount of building time.  Also, there are a lot of options for play after the build is complete.  The bus station complements other City sets (e.g., train station, police station, demolition site, etc.), if you have space for setting up a whole city.

This is a really cute set good sized set (roughly 18 inches long and 4 inches high) made of rugged plastic and with real rubber tires.  The truck looks realistic with nice details inside the cab with a center console, two bucket seats, a dashboard and a steering wheel.  It is molded in 4 colors plus a clear plastic windshield, sunroof and back window.  The side doors have no windows.  The steering wheel turns, but is not functional for turning the wheels.  The two front doors of the truck both open.  The hood of the truck opens to reveal an engine.  Nice detail including non-functioning headlights, parking lights, front grill, side mirrors, gas cap and a rear tailgate that also opens.  The only assembly required on the truck is putting on the 4 tires, which should be easy for just about any kid.  The horse trailer also requires putting on 4 tires and a special wheel in the front; also there are 6 trailer pieces that have to be snapped in place. An illustrated book shows how to install the pieces.  Pretty easy, but a younger child might have a little trouble figuring it out.  The pictures on the side of the trailer and the license plates on the truck and trailer are provided in a sheet of stickers, which a child can place where they like.  Includes a completely black horse with a blanket, 2 figures (a driver for the truck and a handler for the horse) and several accessories for the horse including a bridle, halter, saddle, a couple brushes, a bale of hay and a bunch of carrots.  Suitable for both boys or girls, especially those who like horses.  Probably this toy would be preferred by girls since the truck is pink and the horse handler is female. Even some adult collectors might like this set as it is colorful, detailed and well made. The real downside that I see to this set is that if you have very young children who will be around this, they could easily put one of the small pieces in their mouth and choke.  There is no instructions on showing how to use the horse accessories; most are obvious, but a couple are not.  The most important thing to know is that this is more of an "old school" type of toy; no batteries, no motors, no remotes.  A lot of kids today want the latest electronic devices and radio controlled toys, but this toy allows them to use their imaginations a little. I think that's a plus, but if you buy this for the kid who wants a cell phone instead, there will be a level of disappointment, I'm sure.  Note that this is made by a German company, with components from Germany, China, Romania and Tunisia. It's not that important to me where it is made, but it might be to you.  Note too that you can also buy other items in this series, such as a barn type structure, a corral and other horses.  If your child insists on other components, it could get expensive, if you give in.  As a kid at heart, I like this toy a lot myself and would keep it if I didn't have so many other things.  It will probably go to a grandson.

The LEGO UNIKITTY DR. FOX LABORATORY BUILDING KIT (359 Piece) is an inexpensive LEGO set (about $27 on Amazon) that has a lot of play potential.  With 359 pieces, it takes a while to build the laboratory of Unikitty’s resident mad scientist (Dr. Fox), and there are many interesting features (e.g., the little “refrigerator” with the transparent door, and its contents).  Taking center stage is the “sparkle matter” scanner from the second episode in the Unikitty cartoon series.  It’s at the left side, and next to it is a little set of white rollers that make a conveyor belt for jars filled with sparkle matter.  At the far right is a turntable for filled sample jars that moves when you push it.  The building techniques are easy, and the instructions are clear, so young children should be able to assemble the set.  There are a couple of stickers (Dr. Fox’s blackboard, and her instrument panel) that will probably need adult assistance in applying.  The faces of the figures are printed on the bricks (no stickers required).  The laboratory has a two big clamps that can hold a figure, an examination table (ditto), and various mysterious lamps and instruments.  The set includes Princess Unikitty, Prince Puppycorn, Dr. Fox, Richard, and a robot helper.  This Unikitty building set is a lot of fun (especially for an adult Unikitty fan like me).

Generally I prefer LEGO building kits that aren’t targeted for girls, but Unikitty is so cute, I love the cartoon and this charming kit.  The LEGO UNIKITTY PARTY TIME BUILDING KIT (214 Piece) takes less than an hour for an adult to build, but has some interesting building techniques for young children.  For example, the boxes for the two birthday gifts are constructed from slightly different LEGO elements--teaching that there can be more than one way to build something.  I particularly like the Hawkodile figure, which has a specialized brick for the head with a protruding yellow hawk’s bill.  The faces for Unikitty, Puppycorn, and Master Frown are printed on the specialized bricks for their heads.  The heads on all but Master frown can be turned for play.  The birthday cake is on a trolley with wheels (like roller skates).  The round plate in front spins in a full circle to imitate a dance floor, and will hold one figure at a time. The only possible negative about this set is that there are four stickers (gift box bows and “Party Time”) that small children will need adult help with.  My kit had quite a few extra small pieces.  Perhaps these are included to simulate sparkle.  The kit will make you smile.

[[VIDEOID:51e7617cadf31bd8d128cb7d9c7b5e17]]It's hard to believe that a mere 1000-piece jigsaw puzzle can be so difficult!  The RAVENSBURGER TITANIC PUZZLE is a genuine challenger--a puzzle that's interesting to assemble as a test of an expert jigsawer's skill, but one that's NOT fun to put together, because it's so very, very difficult.  Even the borders are difficult, and it's a temptation to quit altogether when the "easy" parts are completed, and only the top one-third with the dark blue, starry sky remains.  The puzzle painting captures the very essence of the Titanic's last night, showing the doomed ocean liner steaming ahead at top speed on a clear, starry night--through a field of ice, toward the disaster that awaits her.  The dark blues of the sky, the stars in the Milky Way, and the reflections of the ship's lights on the water are so peaceful and beautiful!  This puzzle would be a terrible choice for family entertainment, because the deep-blue sky pieces (in particular) are nearly impossible to distinguish by their colors or starry patterning.  The sky pieces must be laboriously assembled by closely examining the shapes (total number of tabs, large/small tab combinations, left/right offsets for tabs, etc.), and this is a task for a dedicated jigsawer working alone.  Fortunately, it's not easy to make mistakes, because this is a high-quality Ravensburger puzzle with individual pieces that click softly into place.  This makes the assembly job a little bit easier.  (For an equally difficult jigsaw puzzle, one with pieces so similarly shaped that you CAN make mistakes, try the [[ASIN:B00B4RQU5C Tintin Rocket Puzzle]],  if you can find one.)

I decided to test it out again, and it wouldn't turn on. I've charged it for over 5 hours. There is a red light to indicate it's charging, but still won't turn on. No longevity for this item. Reducing review to 1-star.  Original Review: ------------------ Very fun light saber! Has a good heft to it and feels high quality. The handle is some sort of aluminum or metal. This looks much better than those plasticky toys. But as usual, with all these sabers on the market, the worst looking part is the the plastic tube. Our tech isn't there yet, but hopefully one day, we can get rid of that plastic tube and have a real solid light beam out! In any case, here are the controls:  -Single click button once to turn on. -Single click button again to hear effect. -While it's on, Double click for a blasting sound which the manual calls "lockup mode." It will stay at this sound until you Double Click again to get out of it. -While it's on, triple click to change colors (this is finicky though) -Press & hold button to turn off. -To change the sound "theme," while it's off, hold the button, you will hear a sound theme. Hold the button again to hear the next sound theme, hold it again to hear the next, etc. There is a total of 4 themes, one of which is actually mute. -To use any of these themes, once you like what you hear, just single click the button to turn on and play. Remember clicking once turns it on, but holding the button would cause it to cycle through the themes. But once on, to get back to cycling through the themes, you need to press & hold to turn off first, then press & hold to cycle through the themes.  My kids have a few sabers from the out of business toy store. This one belongs to daddy. LOL! I'm knocking 1 star for the finicky controls and instructions that were a little hard to figure out. 4 stars.

Hasbro's done a nice job with the entry into their line of Black Series wearable helmets.  Hasbro addressed some of the criticisms of the Black Series Stormtrooper helmet, which looked nice, but was a difficult fit for some, and the voice electronics weren't very good.  In this Poe helmet there are no plastic straps.  Instead there are 3 separable foam cloth-covered stitched pads (2 ear muffs and an upper piece).  The cloth of the pads stick to any of the 7 built-in velcro discs inside the helmet.  It's a tight fit for me with all the pads installed, but if you detach and remove the ear muffs, you'll get about an extra 2 inches of room in width.  So while this helmet looks a bit smaller than the stormtrooper helmet to me, it is actually roomier inside and should fit most adults.  With the ear muffs removed I can even wear the helmet with glasses on.  There are three speakers inside the helmet:  two at the front top and one in the back rear.  A recessed microphone is at the inside front left.  The external swing-out mic is just for show, there are no electronics in it.  There is no voice amplification in this one instead your voice simply activates one of a set of pre-recorded sounds that include bb-8, X-Wing and Tie Fighter engine sounds, laser blasts and explosions, that are all mixed together in surround sound.  With no voice activation you'll hear a steady in-flight engine sound.  It all gives you an impression that you're in the middle of a starfighter battle.  And it works pretty well.  You can also trigger sounds by pressing the outside disc on the right side of the helmet.  The yellow visor raises up-and-down and has detents to hold it in position.  However, the blast shield above it is fixed.  The paint colors, graphics, and light weathering of the helmet are well done.  The outer dimensions of the helmet in inches are approximately 11x9x12 (width, height, depth).

I selected this game for my young cousin Brandon, who is just a few weeks shy of five.  I am happy to report that he loves this game and has played it 6 times in less than 24 hours!  The object of the game is for the dog, Shelby, to steal as many bones as possible.  The bones are made of rubber, not plastic, and the dog dishes are sturdy cardboard (which may or may not hold up, given that many pre-schoolers are hard on games and toys.  However, the game is designed to teach counting and simple math skills, and it does this quite well.  Since the dog steals bones from other dishes, the children learn to do simple addition and subtraction.  At the end, children count the number of bones Shelby has stolen, practicing their counting skills in a fun way.  I recommend this game to anyone who is looking for a fairly sturdy educational game that a pre-schooler will love.  A testimony from Brandon's mom, Laura:  "I love this game!"

I really like TOMAT and STARTRC products. I have a nice assortment of accessories from this company. However, this is one that disappointed me.  I just got my Air 2s FMC. The OEM case for the combo is very compact. But, not very user friendly or safe for the combo for that matter. Everything is just crammed in.  I ordered this carrying case, thinking I would have room for the complete FMC. I was wrong. The problem is the size. I compared it to their bag for the Mini. It's the same bag. They just changed out the foam insert. As in their pictures, there is room for the drone, the controller and three batteries. That's it. Everything else has to go into that open pouch inside the lid, the flat zippered pocket on the outside of the lid or that tiny elastic pouch on the side. There just is not room for things like the lens filters, the wall charger or the three battery charging base plate. And, all of your other accessories are just jumbled up.  I like TOMAT. I like STARTRC. They have great products. And, if you have the basic Air 2 package, not the FMC, this bag will do just fine. But, the FMC requires a lot more space and better organization.  Two stars.

All of the fossils were in a single spot, and it took only a second to find them. I soaked the block in water, as I didn't want to take hours to dig them out, and after a little bit, I was able to pull the fossils out of the block with ease. The other fossils are all fine, and if you don't care at all about the trilobite, then I guess this kit is still ok to get. This FRED “MARSHMALLOWS” JIGSAW PUZZLE (Dan Saelinger, Artist; 500 Pieces) is a high-quality, fully-interlocking jigsaw puzzle.  It’s mostly made from recycled material, and printed with soy-based ink (both very good qualities in a cardboard product that will be used once or twice and then discarded in the trash).  The puzzle is made of cardboard that is thinner than the cardboard in a Ravensburger jigsaw puzzle.  However, although Ravensburger puzzles represent the gold standard, the cardboard in the FRED puzzles is heavy enough to afford a quite acceptable puzzle-assembly experience.  As a fan of “expert” jigsaws that are challenging to assemble, I really love the “Marshmallows” picture.  There are a lot of straight lines to help with assembly, but you have to look closely at the black areas on the individual puzzle pieces to place the individual marshmallows in their proper locations.  For a picture like this one, a 500-piece puzzle is difficult enough to challenge even the most experienced jigsaw puzzle enthusiast.  The puzzle comes in a sturdy, attractive storage box with a tight-fitting lid.  The box will stack easily, and won’t come open and spill the pieces if you drop the box.

[[VIDEOID:1f2a3c880a660ba73bcdaf865374ae12]] How to set up your Syma X1 before first flight.  I go through the following steps quickly in the video:  1) Place a charged LiPo battery into the slide rails on the bottom of the copter, but not all the way in. 2) Place the copter in normal top-side-up position on a flat surface. 3) THEN push in the copter battery completely.  The red LED in the copter will flash (4 Hz).  Do not move the copter from the flat surface. 4) The flashing stops for about 2 seconds.  I believe at this point the initialization of gyroscopes, stabilization algorithms, etc. has completed.  The copter has just learned where "level" is for this flight. 5) Next the red LED the copter will flash quickly.  If you have not yet bound the copter to the transmitter, the following steps must be completed while the copter is flashing quickly to bind the copter and the transmitter.  If binding has already been completed previously, timing is not important (transmitter can be turned on any time after the fast flashing starts) but the steps are the same to complete the connection between copter and transmitter:   a) Start with transmitter off and the throttle (left stick) all the way down.   b) If you want to switch from Mode 1 to Mode 2 (or from Mode 2 to Mode 1), push the fine tuning switch below the right joystick to the right as you turn on the transmitter.   c) Turn on power to the transmitter.   d) Push the throttle (left stick) all the way up then all the way down.  You will hear a long beep from the transmitter.  The binding between the copter and the transmitter performed in step 5 seems to be persistent across copter and transmitter power cycles and battery changes.  The transmitter also maintains Mode and Trim settings across power cycles and battery changes.  After step 5 you will know the copter and the transmitter are communicating correctly when lights are on steady (no flashing) on both the copter (red) and on the transmitter (blue).  Steps 1-4 must ALWAYS be done before each flight.  If the copter darts off in random directions (especially after performing a level rotation-only maneuver), there's a good chance it's because the gyros weren't initialized properly.  The key is to place the copter on a level surface BEFORE inserting the battery.  I've given the Syma X1 4 stars because it's a lot of fun, but 1 star deducted because the instructions are unclear, and in the case of how to set up the copter preflight, just plain wrong.

This BARBIE 2017 HOLIDAY TERESA DOLL (Brunette) has beautiful brunette hair, with light brown highlighting to add interest.  The full-skirted, full-length gown, made of dark red, sparkly metallic fabric, is to die for, with the skirt slit to above the knee.  The earrings are lovely (though they don't actually move like dangly earrings, because they're made in one piece from plastic).  The doll is intended more for display by collectors than for play.  Both arms move at the shoulder joint, but the left arm is permanently bent into a hand-on-the-hip pose.  The hair is very fragile--some of it started to fly away even as I was removing the doll from the box.  The doll comes with a nice little stand.  The giant star has costume jewelry “rubies” set into the points--very pretty.  The star attaches to the gown with two prongs that fit into loops on the back of the bodice.  (The star doesn't quite work as a tree ornament, because of the prongs.)  Removing the doll from the box is a job for an older child or an adult.  The cardboard backing (carrying the doll) is held inside the clear plastic display box with plastic tabs that must be flattened to release the backing.  Both the skirt of the gown and the doll’s long hair are sewn to the cardboard backing.  The doll’s head, waist, hands, and feet are secured by clear plastic bands and tabs that are taped to the backing.  Clipping all of these “restraints” requires fine scissors, a good eye, and a steady hand to avoid damaging the hair or gown.

This LEGO BAT-DUNE BUGGY 70918 BUILDING KIT (198 pieces, tied in with the LEGO Batman Movie) is about a 45-minute build for someone who’s already familiar with the LEGO parts commonly used in vehicles.  With 198 pieces, there are a lot of parts to identify, so it will take longer for anyone new to LEGO.  It’s a highly varied, satisfying build, even for an adult.  There is quite a bit of sideways building, and there are some combinations that take some attention to figure out.  As always, the instructions are clear.  The finished Bat-Dune Buggy shoots LEGO dots from side-mounted guns.  There is a rubber-band suspension that is activated by pressing down on the tail fins. It’s a fairly solid build, though the back section can pop off if you press too hard.  The buggy has big wheels that turn.  (You may need to turn the front wheels by hand at first to loosen them up. The front wheels are not mounted on an axle like the back wheels.)  There are two minifigs, Batman and Boomerang Man.  Batman’s head has two faces with different expressions; the minifig has a marvelous fabric cape.  Priced about $16 on Amazon, I think that this set returns good value for the money.  When finished, the Bat-Dune Buggy is fun to play with.

[[VIDEOID:a7abc24222a8ac6ac6e109fd1d38813d]] This COOL BAKER CHOCOLATE TREATS MAKER is one of the worst toys marketed for girls that I've ever encountered.  One, it isn't fun (too many "wait several minutes" steps, and needs a lot of adult help).  Two, there is no suitable chocolate included in the box.  Three, it's extremely hard to create the candies as shown in the advertising (even though you're given the molds).  Four, it cannot be taken apart (for flat storage) once it has been assembled.  Five, it's hand-washable and even requires a brush for cleaning the crevices.  MELTING CHOCOLATE.  Much to my surprise, this toy DOES melt chocolate with hot tap water alone.  (At least if your household has very hot tap water like mine does.)  You fill the pink warming tank (which has a separator that can be added for melting different chocolate colors at the same time) with hot water.  Then it takes 5-10 minutes, per the instructions, to melt the chocolate (even if you chop and stir with the spatula as you wait).  If you must actually heat the water for the tank, you must keep the water under a maximum 111 degrees Fahrenheit (well below boiling or simmering), because that is all that the plastic tank can handle.  The instructions warn that an adult should perform the task of filling the chocolate-warming tank.  Different kinds of chocolate do take different amounts of time to melt.  My idea of using butterscotch chips, for the caramel color, turned out to be a bad one.  Butterscotch chips take much longer than milk chocolate chips or white-melting-chocolate disks, and the melted butterscotch isn't very workable even after it's melted.  I was unable to find any colored chocolate disks (as shown in the advertising) for melting, but maybe these are available online or in cooking specialty stores.  CREATING TWO-COLOR CANDIES.  The 10-minute wait for melting isn't too bad.  While you're waiting, you can oil your candy molds.  If you want to create two-color chocolates, you must "paint" the details (owl eyes, gift package ribbon, central heart) into the molds with your first color, and then harden the details in the freezer for another 5 minutes.  Trying to paint in the details was a disaster for me!  Even though I read the instructions, I couldn't see what I needed to do to add the details and get well-defined owl eyes, or anything else.  After you harden the details, you fill the molds with the rest of your chocolate, snap the molds shut, and wipe off any oozing chocolate.  You click each mold into the spinner thingy, and spin it for about 1 minute, before putting the mold into the freezer for a final 15 minutes to harden the candy.  I don't know what the spinning is for--maybe it helps eliminate air bubbles--but I do know that spinning isn't fun.  It's just boring.  After 15 minutes of freezing, you can unmold your chocolates fairly easily.  They will probably have edges that need to be knocked off, because the chocolate oozes onto the flat parts of the mold when the mold is snapped shut.  AMOUNT OF CHOCOLATE NEEDED.  An unpleasant surprise for me, was that a warming tray full of melted chocolate was NOT enough to fill all three candy molds.  It was only enough to fill 1 to 1-1/2 candy molds.  I had to melt chocolate in the largest section of the warming tank TWICE more to fill all three molds.  (And being an adult, I didn't spill or waste any melted chocolate to speak of.)  GIFT TAGS AND BAGS.  The full toy kit includes a spinner thingy, three candy molds, three tools (a two-ended scoop, a "paintbrush" for details, a useful spatula), some small plastic gift bags, and a sheet of gift stickers.  The gift tags and bags are a nice idea, but you will have to practice with the molds if you want to create giftable candies.  The whole process is, of course, very inefficient, and practicing will require a lot of melting of chocolate and washing of molds.  It took me a couple of hours to go through everything for the first time.  ASSEMBLY.  Probably the best thing about this toy is that it snaps together easily, and assembles into a solid "spinner" device.  However, the snap-together parts expand behind the openings they're pushed through (sort of like toggle bolts).  This means that the parts cannot be pulled apart again, for flat storage, once they've been connected.  (I couldn't pry them apart with a screwdriver.)  This is a space-hogging toy.  CLEANING.  The parts of the toy are hand washable only.  Chocolate sticks in the crevices of the molds and the chocolate-warming tank, and doesn't come out with soaking, so that a dish brush is required for cleaning.  SUMMATION.  This toy will allow a parent and child to create candies from melted chocolate, but there is little educational value.  Making candies this way bears no relationship to how candy is ordinarily made in the home kitchen.  Also, making candies this way isn't fun.  Most of the process involves waiting for chocolate to melt  or waiting for chocolate to harden in the freezer.  It's very difficult to fill the molds with the precision needed to create candies that resemble the ones shown on the box. Thus the finished candies are likely to be disappointing.  The finished candies taste like what they're made of--melted and hardened chocolate chips.  Because this toy does actually melt chocolate chips or melting chocolate disks, and allow them to be molded into candies, as advertised, I rate it, rather generously, at 2 stars ("I don't like it" on the official Amazon scale).

Toddlers love to play and they benefit from toys that encourage learning or help in other areas of development. One toy that seems promising in these ways is the B’S Eye View Peg Puzzle Dexterity Toy – Classic Wooden Puzzle for Toddlers.  This puzzle has knobs on the pieces, making it easy for toddlers to grab each piece. It helps with coordination, too, as little ones will learn to fit the right shape into the right slot. However, the colors of the pieces are washed out and they really need to be an eye catching primary. Not only would it be more inviting to toddlers and preschoolers but it would provide an opportunity to not only teach numbers and counting, but color recognition too while playing.  Another issue with the pieces is that the details are lacking. If you are going to add detail make it something a child can recognize. But probably the most shocking oversight is the shapes and numbers that they are supposed to match. There is an octagon, for example, that represents the number 7, a five- pointed star that is used for the number 8, and so on. Why would you do this? The octagon should be the number 8 and the star should match with number 5. This is an oversight that someone should have caught.  Other issues I have are with the puzzle holes- they are a little too big and this would be hard to travel anywhere with. I do like that the numbers are nice and big and easy to see and that the knobs are big and tall enough for tiny fingers to grasp. Nah, I would pass on this. Normally, I would say the end result doesn't matter, it's the process and the family time that counts, but even the process was basically nothing. All you do is stir the crystal powder in boiling water until it all melts. That's it, nothing else for the kids to do. Sit back and wait for the underwhelming results. The crystal forms  a square at the base, so you'll need to break it up in order to fit it on the circular display. The black base and clear dome display are cheap thin plastic. The kind of plastic that is used in packaging that you would normally throw away. The crystal cannot stay still on the base because the dome does not clip onto the base tightly. It just covers the base loosely, so if it moves, the crystal inside moves. We threw away the dome lids, and simply flip the base upside down to use as a tray to hold the crystal, and all it's broken crumbs. That's another thing, it's very brittle. The Science is interesting though. I think I'm most annoyed at the display cases actually. The stock photos make it seem like you're getting glass display cases, or at least acrylic, and the black base looks like hard plastic. What you get is the type of thin plastics used as tray liners in packaging, the flimsy stuff you throw away. 2-Star.

My great-grandson liked this a lot and really enjoyed riding it.  He's not a large kid, but is one year older than the recommended ages for the toy.  He is within the weight limit, which I think is 50 pounds or under.  He can probably enjoy it for about a year, but he was almost too big for it now so you probably don't want to get this for a child who is approaching the upper age limit, which I think the box said was 5.  Also, if you have a big child close to the maximum age and maximum weight, they might not fit the toy.  At around a $200 price, I consider this to be a luxury toy and you might be able to find toys for a lot less that would be just as much fun for a kid.  I recommend getting it for kids at the lower end of the age range (2 or 3), so they can enjoy it for a few years before they outgrow it.  Of course you can always donate it when they do outgrow it.  Not much to assemble on it and the few parts that need to be put on are easy to install.  The only real downside to this toy is the initial charge time of 18 hours.  Kids are impatient (some adults are too) and if you present this to them and then they find out they can't use it until the next day, there could be some disappointment.  Of course that could be a way to teach patience too.  After the initial overnight charge, the battery seemed to last quite well, so they should be able to enjoy a driving session until they lose interest and a recharge is needed.  This is not huge, but it is a large item that you need a storage place for and a place to plug it in while it is charging.  As several reviewers have said, this works better on hard surfaces.  This doesn't move very fast, but fast enough for younger kids; older kids might find it too slow.  Obviously too, as a kid gets older they might not find a toy like this that "cool".  Since this is a motorized riding toy, obviously you do need to supervise a kid while riding it, especially younger ones; don't buy it if you think you are going to turn your kid loose with it and not have to watch them.  You need adequate space in your house or a big driveway to use this, as you probably don't want the kids driving it in the street, unless you live in a very low traffic residential area and/or are right beside them when they are using it.  Probably best for use on sidewalks or a fenced yard if you are going to use it outside.  Keep in mind too that if you go any great distance away from your house and the charge runs out, you now have a 50 pound toy to somehow get home.  How noisy a toy is also can be a factor on whether you want to get it or not; this runs reasonably quiet, which is a plus.  A fun toy for the appropriately aged small child.

This HOT WHEELS TRACK BUILDER SYSTEM STUNT KIT is one of the best "stunt kits" yet, because it can be set up in any of four (4) different gravity-defying configurations.  You build a basic "stunt base", then choose the build that suits your numbered experience level:  High Jump (1), Criss Cross (2), Long Jump (3), or The Drop (4).  If you tire of one of the builds, you can easily take it apart and rebuild it into a different one.  (The stunt pictured on the box is the High Jump.)  Because I'm experienced with the Track Builder System, I chose to build the Long Jump.  (I passed on The Drop for now, because it requires a tabletop as well as floor space for the setup.)  This is a high-performance stunt--you load a car into the rubber-band-powered launcher, hit the launcher release disk hard, and send the car up a ramp, around a loop-de-loop, over a wide gap to a ramp landing, and then down the track to exit the stunt into the rest of your Track Builder layout.  You can use the "measuring stick" to measure and lengthen the gap (the distance to the ramp),  once you master the launching technique needed to send the car through the entire stunt to a successful completion.  Unfortunately, this stunt kit is also one of the most difficult to build.  The Hot Wheels instructions are always a bit confusing--the drawings are three-dimensional, but they lack the dimensional accuracy of LEGO drawings.  The parts are shown in the instructions in their actual plastic colors, which helps, but the drawings don't always make it easy to identify the shapes of the parts or to see exactly how they go together.  However, the Building Instructions and parts for this kit have a feature I don't remember seeing before:  letters (A, B, C, etc.) molded into the parts, so you can match the letters (A to A; B to  B: etc.) to connect the parts.  This, too, helps with the building stage.  (Always remember that if you can't get the car to complete the stunt--no matter how hard you try--you probably made a mistake in the build.)  The parts in the "stunt base" (see my first photo below) click together very tightly, and more or less permanently.  I was unable to pry the parts apart, even using workshop tools.  As a result, the assembled stunt kit cannot be taken completely apart for flat storage in the original box.  This stunt kit comes with only one Hot Wheels car, and you will need at least one more car to fully play with it.  All four configurations have suggestions for using two cars (crashing at track crossings,  comparing speeds, etc.).  The kit has two (2) extremely powerful 4-speed launchers--I love these!  The launchers are the best I've ever seen.  (They are already set up when you take them out of the box--don't fool with the rubber bands.)  Another new wrinkle in this Stunt Kit is that you can use the retail box itself to add interest and difficulty to your larger Track Builder System layout.  There are printed ramps and "fire pits" that can be pushed up and locked together to make a platform with more obstacles for the cars.  This is an excellent choice for any Track Builder System layout, because of its versatility, economical use of floor space, and low price (about $24 on Amazon).  The only drawback is that it's harder to build than most Hot Wheels stunts.

I had the luck of visiting Portofino last October, and was instantly hooked on this 1,000-piece Ravensburger puzzle that perfectly captures the vibrant colors and Mediterranean atmosphere of the town. The framing of the photo is interesting as well, with several moored boats in the foreground and the harbor in the background. The level of detail in the high-resolution photo is amazing (the finished puzzle measures 27 X 20). The puzzle pieces are sturdy (unlike some cheaper puzzles I've assembled) and nonglare.  This is a fabulous challenge to have on hand for weekends or relaxation; the large amount of blue in the water and sky make these more difficult sections to accomplish, but the end result is well worth the effort!

This LEGO FRIENDS EMMA’S PLAY CUBE 41404 BUILDING KIT (New 2020 (36 Pieces)) will be appreciated by any kid or adult who loves miniatures and dollhouses.  It isn’t much of a building kit, because there are only 36 pieces (including the doll, the surprise animal, the mirror, the hairbrush, the scissors, etc.).  Because it contains only a few classic LEGO elements, any creative play must come from doll play rather than from rebuilding the elements into something different.  Of the three play cubes that I’ve assembled, Emma’s is my least favorite.  The cube depicts a pet grooming and photography studio, so the furnishings are kind of static.  There is a mirror, a little laptop computer (a sticker gives it a display showing a choice of styles for the “client”), a camera on a stand, and a table for a pet to sit on during a photography session.  The right-hand half of the cube has a great background sticker showing a window with a view of the Eiffel Tower.  The “surprise” consists of a cat figure in one of four colors (red, teal, gold, lavender).  I love the fact that this series has the same “surprise” animal in each themed cube, the “surprise” being the color of the animal.  This way there is no real pressure to “collect them all“ when it comes to the “surprises”.

I was looking for a DotA / MOBA in board game format and this fits the bill pretty nicely.  It's quite expensive and also a massively complex game. Not for the faint of heart. If you want super premium components, you get what you pay for. Comes with cases and shelves for all the components, and the art on the neoprene mats and the cards is gorgeous. I'm quite happy with it, but it's one of those "big box" games that you only need one or maybe two of in your entire collection.  As a game it feels a bit different than League of Legends (as it should given the format), but it's just something to be aware of and expect. I had been designing my own DotA board game and it's not exactly what I had in mind, but it's definitely fun.  The biggest drawback is the learning time and the playing time. I wouldn't even consider doing more than 2 players unless everyone in the group had already played several 2 player games and knew the rules well. It could really benefit from simultaneous turns and I'll be thinking up some house rules for sure to make the playthroughs not take quite so long.  The races are pretty asymmetric which some people might not like. You need to learn one race and stick with it. The complexity is far too big to bounce around and try each race in turn. Each race's choice of minions, spires, heroes, and faction upgrades has a LOT of different options and it could be pretty overwhelming at first. Learning all the different abilities, how they work, and figuring out how to work that into your strategy and gameplay.

UPDATE 08-04-2018:  My mistake re the leaflet link for the instructions--the ending after the dot is "co", not "com", so the link as printed does exist.  It doesn't change my star rating, though.  An adult or child still must create a profile on the kit manufacturer's site to be able to view the "thirsty plant" instructions.  In a high-priced kit (about $34 on Amazon), the instructions belong in the box. ----- The first strike against this TECH WILL SAVE US THIRSTY PLANT KIT (Educational STEM Toy, Ages 8 and Up) is that there is NO instruction manual packed in the box.  The second strike is that the printed leaflet actually included in the box gives a web address for obtaining the manual that no longer exists.  (The company domain is available for purchase, as if the company were out of business.)  The third strike is that once you locate the online manual, the thirsty plant kit requires you to cast a columnar sensor from plaster of Paris (what a mess!), but doesn’t include a stirrer or the soft clay needed to close the bottom of the mold during casting.  If you want the instruction manual, you can Google “tech will save us thirsty plant kit”.  It appears that the PDF version of the manual no longer exists.  (At least my browser wouldn’t open the pdf file from the link that Google brought up.)  However, there is an online instruction manual available (as of 08-04-2018) if your child--with adult permission--signs up for a free membership in the kit maker’s “Club Make”.  The third strike is a little unfair of me, in that the product page listing mentions the use of plaster of Paris.  However, if you haven’t made anything with plaster of Paris in years, you will end up with a horrible mess on your work table.  It doesn’t help that the instruction manual says to pour “a thumb” of water into the included dish for mixing, when you actually need about ¼ of that (or about ½ of the amount shown in the photo). I guess the manual means a child’s “thumb”.  If you succeed in making the water sensor--which needs to dry for 24 hours before you can continue assembling the kit--then putting together the circuitry seems fairly simple.  I do wonder whether an 8-year-old can do it without adult help, though.  As far as I can tell, the completed water sensor will work as advertised, and flash a LED light (in the daytime only) when a potted plant’s soil dries out.  Unfortunately, I can’t say for sure that the sensor works, because I bombed out at the sensor-casting stage.

This LEGO JUNIORS ELASTIGIRL’S ROOFTOP PURSUIT BUILDING KIT (10759; 95 Piece) features minifigs (Elastigirl and Screenslaver) from the “Incredibles 2” movie.  Elastigirl is interesting to minfig lovers, because her body element is molded with long tubes (solid plastic) for arms,  instead of having the standard poseable arms.  The set has reasonable play potential, with a helicopter and a motorcycle (the Elasticycle) for zooming around.  Both vehicles have space to accommodate a minifig operator.  A cute graphic element is the hypno-disc on the helicopter’s propeller.  The brick-built scenery (billboard, satellite dish, antenna, and helipad) inspires the acting out of many different creative stories.  LEGO Juniors building kits use standard LEGO pieces, but have slightly simplified building instructions with extra illustrated steps.  The kits also use fewer brick-built components (e.g., a baseplate may come with two levels molded in), and have printed graphics rather than tricky-to-apply stickers.  The bricks may come in more colors, to aid in assembly.

Updated 10/17: See below for update.  My family has been a fan of Settlers of Catan for years. My college kids were excited to give this 3D version a try. Unfortunately, more than half of the pieces were missing. It would be an understatement to say we were disappointed. Especially since we were teased by the few pieces that we did receive. I contacted the customer support for the Catan game. They said they couldn't help me and directed me to the manufacturer. I contacted the manufacturer. They said they couldn't help me either. Our game came with the 19 painted terrain hexes, 6 painted sea frames and 9 painted harbor pieces (I think). We received no cards, no instructions, no dice, no rules booklet, no tokens, no settlements, no roads and possibly more. What we have is unplayable and unusable. And a disappointed family!  Update: 10/17: After I posted my original review, I was contacted by the makers of Catan. They apologized for the poor customer service that was given. They also sent me a replacement game for which my family is very appreciative. The game has arrived in its entirety. The game play with the 3D pieces is awesome! It really adds a whole new element to the game. It does take a lot of room to play, more so than the original, but it's really fun to see and feel the pieces. I think it creates a more immersive experience. My 23 year old son has scheduled an upcoming game night with his friends to play this version of the beloved game.

But they need to credit him. You don't steal from an artist, period.

I love the photograph for this RAVENSBURGER LE MONT-SAINT-MICHEL JIGSAW PUZZLE (1000 Piece), and I love expert challenger jigsaws, but this is the most tedious challenger puzzle that I’ve ever tackled.  The center portion with the monastery buildings and their reflection goes very quickly, but the four outer corners are a total slog.  At least half the puzzle is pale blue or white sky/sea. There is little in the way of color differentiation, so the only way to put the pieces together is to eyeball the shapes.  You can speed this up a little by sorting the blue/white pieces (e.g., 2-tabs, 3-tabs, 1-tab with squarish “foot”, etc.).  But it will still take a long time, and close study of individual pieces, for even an expert jigsaw assembler to complete the picture.  Like all Ravensburger puzzles, this one is high quality, with 1000 totally unique pieces.  The pieces click together smoothly, so you always know immediately when you’ve located the correct piece.  This very difficult puzzle will teach you a lot about the pattern Ravensburger follows in cutting its jigsaws.  If you love jigsaw puzzles for the pure challenge, this is the puzzle for you!

My family absolutely loves Pixar movies and their characters. I thought these would be fun to have on a shelf in my office. The figure talks right out of the box, so there's no need to locate batteries before you start playing with it. There is a button on Frozone's chest that you can press to start talking. The voice has to be the real voice of the character.  If you have another figure from the Interactables line, then they will interact with one another. They have to be facing each other to talk. Some of the phrases seem to be perfect responses, but some do not. They do call each other by name  when conversing. I found that when you press one of the figures to activate, the one that was pushed will say a line. Then the other one replies or responds back. Then the original one says one more line. I have tried that with a few different figures and that applies to all of them that I have tried. Just press one of the buttons at the end of the 3rd phrase and they'll continue with another 3 responses. I do wish that the figures came with stands. They can stand up on a table or shelf, but they're not particularly stable and if you bump the surface, they'll fall down. So far I have been having a lot of fun with these! I can't wait until they release some more characters!

This LEGO DUPLO MY FIRST ALPHABET TRUCK ((10915) New 2020, 36 Pieces) is aimed at very young builders, aged 18 months and up.  The DUPLO elements (NOT compatible with the elements in standard LEGO sets) are very large, designed for toddlers who are still developing their fine motor skills.  The DUPLO truck holds 26 alphabet blocks--each block is printed with a single letter on one side.  This gives you and your child a complete set of alphabet blocks for teaching/learning ABCs.  The blocks are so much more interesting than a “classic” set of alphabet blocks!  They can be stacked like regular blocks, and also loaded on and off the two truck trailers.  The set includes three doll-like play figures:  a girl, a boy, and a teddy bear.  The set also includes a flat sheet with the alphabet blocks printed full-size, in A-Z order, for learning the order of the letters.  Twenty-six of the thirty-six pieces are used for the alphabet blocks.  (There isn’t much assembly required in this set.)  I wish that this delightful, versatile set had been available when I was learning my own ABCs!

I used to make my own greeting cards with rubber stamps, so I was excited to share this craft with my 6 year old niece when she was visiting recently. This is a nice starter set for kids. The set includes pre-mounted wooden stamps, colored pencils, an 2 color ink pad and some outlined stickers of the same stamp images for coloring. The addition of the stickers was a little odd to me, because with the stamps and ink pad, you could basically make your own stickers. The stamps are a good quality. There is excess rubber around the images, so if you aren't careful when stamping, you may get the lines from the edges on your art work. I used to get stamps that had to be assembled, so I always trimmed the rubber around the edges to avoid that. The ink pad is a double one so you can carefully use just one side of the ink pad for a single color or put the rubber into the center and get both colors. There are no papers, cards, etc for stamping on to, so you need to get that. Otherwise, it's a fun activity for kids. My niece is 6 and she had a lot of fun with it.

I'm only giving it two stars because it gives me the giggles making up scatological puns and toilet humor when I look at it or show it to friends otherwise It'd be a solid one (see!) trending down (I can't stop!). I don't know what the final price will be, I've seen it insanely high and just ridiculously high. To be honest, if I saw the in the Dollar Store FOR a dollar and no more I'd buy a dozen and send them to my sillier friends. Beyond a great joke gift this is junk. I doubt it would hold a child's attention for more than a couple minutes tops. It's fairly cheap, thin plastic that topples easily. The seat won't stay up (just like a real toilet). The spring that's supposed to shoot your. . . scat in the air fires the moment you let go of it. There's no way you can press it down, drop in one of the two turds it comes with (see image), close the lid and 'watch it fly' as per the instructions. I've managed the task twice out of dozens of attempts. It's very easy to clog the toilet (see image) and even if you get it right it doesn't fly, it plops (I can't help myself) an inch or two away. The toilet lacks a handle, a small detail but one that would add realism. The figures they come with are sloppily painted. At anything more than a buck it's overpriced and suitable only as a joke gift. Be kind, don't give this to your kids.  Edit 01-22-18: Being serious for a moment here. I wanted to comment on the 'Squishy' description in the product's title. While this company may produce similar toys that are soft and pliable the ones that came with this toilet launcher are NOT. They are hard plastic and a potential choking hazard. The arms on the 'log' are especially fragile and could easily be broken off. I feel the four (4) year old age limitation set by the manufacturer is much too young. Please, use caution when giving this product to younger children. Thank you.

I was a bit crestfallen upon arrival of the Spellbook Cards Holder. It was packaged well, but has apparently had some contact with a moist environment sometime between manufacture and arrival. The leather cord has corroded a metal brad at the spine holding the plastic sleeves in place along with the metal dragon clasp. The leather cord is also much, much too short to pull taut when the Spellbook is full of cards. The leather cover has permanent denting from the tautness simply from holding the included blank cards during shipping. I will be replacing the leather cording myself, and live with the damage.  Damages aside, this is a beautiful Tome of Recollection. It is genuine leather, that is double mounted onto a canvas at the back and two thin leather pieces are joined together canvas side to canvas side. It has 60 sleeves, which should be sufficient for most tabletop players to transport their collection of spells for a single session. I would not suggest trying to stuff more than one card per sleeve, or else risk the plastic sleeves splitting.  Overall, this is a very cool style and the price point makes for a very affordable gift for yourself or any card-welding tabletop player in your life. I do hope my experience is a bit of a fluke, but most vendors can offer a remedy in the event of an unsatisfactory purchase.  Thank you for reading my review. I hope you found it both informative and helpful.

[[VIDEOID:8a35051fba3b622e613e552275a81a53]] This is a great starter drone, in theory. I have flown many of these and my son wanted to try one to get started with. He picked it up really quickly and together we were able to fly it around the house indoors without hitting anything. The camera is gimmicky at best, but for the price I'd say get it for the drone flight fun and forget the camera. UNTIL... within 2 days of owning it, something seemed to go wrong with the height sensor (barometer?), and it not only wouldn't hold the correct height like it did the day before, but suddenly it shot straight UP. No pressing down on the joystick seems to change anything. Its as if the unit felt like it was falling and needed to compensate by flying more aggressively upwards. It hit the ceiling, and we promptly hit the kill switch. I took a look at it, pulled off the propellers to look, and tried again. Same thing - it shot straight UP. Hit the ceiling. I tried cleaning any hair/debris from the blades and again- straight up until it hit. But this time, it started making a funny noise. Upon closer inspection I found one of the pins that the propeller sits on has been bent. I don't know if this was caused by it going straight up or if it caused it, but the drone has been grounded for now. You can see in the attached video how when that pin is spun, it tilts forward and back, rather that the other pins which spin in place. It is possibly that I just got a dud and others would love this, I wouldn't mind trying it again and seeing if I have a different outcome. It did work really well for the first 2 days, and I really like that it not only has a replaceable battery but comes with 2 additional batteries as well! Flight times are usually small on these things so having many extra batteries ready to go is useful. If it hadn't gone nuts and shot upwards everytime, I'd give this thing 5 stars. I'm going to see what I can about sourcing a replacement or additional unit and come back to update this review if it turns out I had a lemon.

When I saw the opportunity to review this Radio Flyer four-in-one tricycle, I was eager to accept:  My cousin and his wife, who live a couple doors down the street, have a three-year-old, and they have just learned that they are expecting another baby in June.  So I knew that this toy would be used in all four of its "incarnations" at one point or another.  The toy arrived within three days, even though I did not request any special shipping status.  The directions for putting the tricycle together are excellent and detailed.  They are entirely pictorial, with no words at all, which is a godsend for those of us who labor for hours trying to read badly translated written instructions.  Laura (the mom) put the entire tricycle together with only minimal help from Brian (the dad).  Brian did have to help with Instruction #13, and also with tightening screws and bolts here and there.  I was delighted to hear that one does not need an engineering degree to put this toy together.  The tricycle went together very easily for the most part.  The handlebars are the hardest part of the construction.  Laura recommends that anyone putting this tricycle together pull up on the handlebars after attaching the bars and the clamp to be sure that the clamp has tightened properly.  She pulled up on the handlebars at least four times and was able to remove them despite that clamp being on.  You just have to tighten the clamp as much as possible.  This tricycle seems very durable, though I will add to this review after Brandon (the three-year-old) has used the toy more.  The canopy is very nice, and the straps on the seat will be great for the new baby when he or she can use it (at nine months or so).  The cupholder that surrounds the infant in the seat will be great for the baby as well.  My only wish is that the tricycle had rubber tires instead of plastic tires.  We live in a somewhat rural area with no sidewalks, and the plastic tires may not hold up as well as rubber tires.  The push handle, to be used for infants, adjusts to the height of the adult pushing the tricycle, which is a wonderful feature.  Now to the acid test:  Does the child like the toy?  Brandon has been off this tricycle only to eat and sleep ever since it was put together.  He even attempted to get on it several times while his mom was putting it together, saying, "I should try it!"  With winter coming on he will probably use it only in the house at first, but Brian and Laura have a huge basement, and riding his tricycle there will give him hours of play time.  I highly recommend this tricycle, especially if your child or children are young enough to take advantage of it in all its formations.  The product description says it can be used from nine months to five years, and that sounds about right.

This LEGO SPIDER MAN FAR FROM HOME: STARK JET AND DRONE ATTACK 76130 BUILDING KIT (504 Pieces) lets kids recreate scenes from the “Spider Man Far From Home” movie, but it’s a great set all by itself. There are enough pieces to make it a satisfying build (moderate difficulty), and it’s fun to play with even if you haven’t seen the movie.  My favorite feature is the bomb hatch located behind the pilot/passenger compartment that seats the minifigs. You can seat Spidey in the hatch, press a button, and let him drop down on his web string to dangle beneath the plane.  You can also use the hatch to drop brick-built energy bombs.  Behind the hatch, there is another compartment that opens for storing bombs and weapons.  The two little drones are quick to build.  The drones and the Stark jet are all equipped with stud shooters.  (Stud shooters are special spring-loaded bricks that shoot off single studs that were loaded onto them, when you move a lever.)  The jet has a minimum of stickers (always a good thing, in my opinion).  The “adjustable wings” are the two upper ones at the tail.  They can be adjusted to different angles.  The big wings at the sides, and their wingtips, are fixed in place.  The kit includes minifigs for Happy Hogan, Nick Fury, Spider-Man, and Mysterio, as well as their associated weapons.

As a child I was a huge fan of Legos and had dozens of sets… Ranging from castles to pirates. However, they’ve been sitting in boxes in the basement for the last 30 years… When I happen to discover Lego‘s new botanical collection at the botanical garden, I had to buy for nostalgia value.  The first thing I immediately noticed is the boxes are much harder to open… I actually had to look on YouTube for how to open it without destroying the box. (hint: run a butter knife along the edge to loosen the glue). Once I got it out of the box, it is self-explanatory, with components labeled in four separate bags and an assembly tool.  It took me around two hours to assemble, a lot of the assembly is repetitive as you are making the same parts over and over for the base, stems, and flowers.  The base is highly interesting as it looks very simple from the outside, but it’s made of rotating parts inside in different colors.  The flower stems can be arranged at different heights and patterns, making it fun to customize. I loved the realistic snaking roots and Dirt toned blocks to fill in around the stems.  I paid $50 for this from the official Lego store and don’t regret it for a second; it looks fantastic on top of my Japanese tea chest and it was a lot of fun. My next challenge is a Lego bonsai!

They are fun and work well, with the Iron Man being my favorite. This glove, however, is very disappointing. Like the other Marvel firing gloves, you have to load it by pulling back on a mechanism and then perform a motion to fire (Black Panther requires a power slash move, Iron man pull your hand back to reveal repulsors, etc).
