[[VIDEOID:1c8150e87e66d2b60e0ec8095157a4f1]] If you’re an adult, this LEGO DUPLO DISNEY FROZEN ICE CASTLE (10899; New 2019; 59 Pieces) takes less than half an hour to assemble, but it’s a terrific set.  DUPLO blocks are intended for very young builders, and are much larger than regular LEGO blocks.  Thus this “Frozen” ice castle set lacks the detail of the regular  [[ASIN:B07TB4WCJM LEGO “Frozen” ice palace]] (see photos).  But this DUPLO set has a light brick!  The light brick is powered by 3 button cell batteries (pre-installed).  When you press the button on the brick, it cycles through a series of colors for a short time, then stops automatically.  By repeatedly pressing the button, you can get all white lights; or all blue, red, yellow, green, purple, orange lights.  The batteries are replaceable, but you will need a small Phillips head screwdriver to open the light brick.  The batteries are NOT accessible to small children.  In comparison to the regular LEGO set, the other great thing about this DUPLO set is the size of the play figures.  Elsa, Anna, and Olaf are much larger than regular LEGO mini-figures, so they’re more fun to play with or display.  Elsa and Anna have skirts with elastic waists that go on over the plastic figures.  Elsa has a sparkly cape, and Anna has a dark red cloak.  Olaf is just himself!  It’s kind of amazing, how much the look and feel of the LEGO “Frozen” ice palace set is retained in this simplified DUPLO set.

Hasbro’s Beyblade Burst Evolution battling tops are fun toys, but they have a learning curve.  The included instructions are so brief as to be almost useless, though they do explain how to assemble the top.  (There are detailed instructions and videos available on the toy manufacturer’s web site.  Go to the official Hasbro site, choose “Brands”, and then “Beyblade Burst”.)  The back of the toy card gives details on the features (e.g., type (attack or defense), weight, endurance, etc.) of the included top.  This BEYBLADE BURST EVOLUTION SWITCHSTRIKE STARTER PACK (Wyvron W3) contains a Wyvron W3 (a right-spin top), and a launcher that can be used for either left-spin or right-spin tops.  You will need to purchase a “battle stadium” separately (e.g., [[ASIN:B01F6VS3ZG Beyblade Burst Beystadium]]).  These tops require two people and two tops for play, because the tops are intended to be launched simultaneously for each battle.  My efforts to launch two tops in close succession weren’t very successful, and the resulting battles weren’t very satisfying.  It took some practice to get a good “Rip” (launch).  It also took me awhile to figure out that the launcher wouldn’t work unless the strip was inserted in the correct slot (e.g., right for a right-spin top), that the top needed to be rotated slightly onto the launcher, and that the tops were meant to be dropped into the stadium (not started on the stadium floor).  (I thought originally that the starter-pack launcher would give either a right or a left spin to any Beyblade top.)  The parts for these Burst Evolution tops can be interchanged to create custom tops with different strengths and weaknesses.  I would strongly advise any adult who is purchasing one of these tops for a youngster to find out exactly which model it is that the kid wants.  The tops perform very differently, depending on their weight and on features such as the type of tip (e.g., rollerball).  Hasbro’s original price for these tops was apparently about $13.  Prices are higher now, and vary quite a bit among online sellers.

I gave the Colorama game to my cousin for her four-year-old, and I am happy to say that he loves this game and asks repeatedly to play it.  Essentially, this game is designed to teach a young child shapes and colors, and it has several levels of play, from very basic to a bit more complex.  You can play by concentrating on shapes only, on colors only, or on a combination of the two.  The game is fun for kids, and if Brandon is any example, they will like it even if they already have a good grasp of shapes and colors.  Brandon has a younger brother, Joey, who  at 10 months is too young for the game,  but I can see this as a game the two could play together in about a year, with Brandon "teaching" Joey all the ins and outs of shapes and colors.  This game is a good use of time and money,  and I recommend it to anyone looking for a fun way to reinforce the basics of shapes and colors.

I bought two of these sets, one for a six-year-old, tho other for a twelve-year-old.  The six-year-old liked the toy well enough, but he needed a lot of help in putting some of the projects together.  He loved the flying saucer project and set it off several times in a row.  The 12-year-old, however, was completely entranced.  He opened the box and immediately set about making a doorbell, a radio, a flying saucer, a Morse code program, a trombone, a typewriter, and several other projects--all before the end of his birthday party!.  His sisters, one 14 and the other 12, were also entranced and had to be reminded this was their brother's gift.  I do not recall ever giving a gift that has been this much of an immediate "hit."  One thing I wondered about:  Are the kids really learning about electronics, or are they just following the diagrams?  But I think that this approach is similar to the way kids learn a video game or a computer program:  They do not read about it and analyze it; they just do it, and learn in the doing.  If you ask as they are making a project,  "Can you think of an application for that?", the learning will be enhanced.  I recommend this toy highly, and I am thinking of ordering the Snap Circuits 750 as a gift for all three kids for Christmas.

I like the Trackmaster series.  Some of the tank locs my children have a real diehards.  Still, when I went to get more at Walmart, I ended up returning several multiple times before I got good ones.  The problem with Thomas was the frame was twisted so that when he rolled down the tracks, only three wheels touched the track.  One of the front wheels kind of rode up in the air.  If you pushed down on him, he would rocker over onto that wheel.  The front axels are split.  There's two spindles instead of a solid axle across the front.  Looking at my old Stepney engine, it has a solid axle.  The solid axle requires the manufacturer to assemble it and swage a cap on the end of the pin.  With the current Thomas design, they just pop two independent spindles on each side of the plastic frame.  It's cheaper to make.  The problem is, if the plastic frame isn't straight (not a chance) the pins will be out of alignment and so will the wheels.  The design is made cheap to make as if the Chinese labor isn't cheap enough already.  If Fisher Price wants to go this cheap, they're going to have to discard a lot more of the flawed units which makes their cost go back up.  Instead they decided to make the consumer lose.

[[VIDEOID:5795137e2a23bd3d76a56d2b9446c889]] If you’re already familiar with K’Nex building sets, this K’NEX THRILL RIDES-REVOLUTION FERRIS WHEEL is a simple, fast build.  The Ferris wheel is big, but it doesn’t require a lot of pieces.  Also, much of the assembly involves building duplicate parts that combine to make the wheel and its swinging gondolas.  The set is recommended for age 7+, and that sounds reasonable (assuming that an adult provides some initial help with making the connections, and assists with lifting the wheel onto the axle).  Each shape of piece is a different color, which detracts from the appearance of the finished Ferris wheel, but makes the construction simpler.  There aren’t many “snowflake” connections, which I usually have to puzzle over to get the right orientation.  (K’Nex pieces are much harder to work with than LEGO pieces.)  The instructions are reasonably clear.  This set took me about 5 hours to build (from opening the box to completion, working at a leisurely pace).  Unfortunately, the finished “thrill ride” isn’t very thrilling.  Like a full-sized amusement park ride, the Ferris wheel doesn’t do much—it just goes around and around in a circle.  Also, it does NOT have a variable speed control—you cannot start, stop, or speed up the wheel gradually (like a real ride).  Although the motor has a single speed, it does run in forward and reverse directions.  The motor is powered by two AA batteries (not included).  For the Ferris wheel to turn properly, one edge of the inner wheel track must be settled into the groove on the “gear wheel” attached to the motor.  You can easily fiddle with the base to adjust things so that the track and groove always make good contact.  Because it’s sort of boring and lacks a variable speed control, I rate this K’Nex Ferris Wheel kit at 4 stars (“I like it” on the official Amazon scale).

This HOT WHEELS SHIFTERS COLOR SPLASH SCIENCE LAB PLAYSET is my new favorite Hot Wheels Stunt accessory!  It's messy, because the car splashes water when it lands in a pool, but it's fun.  You load the tank at the top with ice water, and the pool at the end of the track with hot water, and the included Color-Shifter car will change color when it comes in contact with either the hot or the cold water (dark to light color, or vice versa).  My Color-Shifter car needs to park underneath the cold waterfall (below the tank) or the cold spray (the pipe extending from the water tank) for a little while in order to change color, but there are moveable barriers that allow parking.  The Color-Shifter car changes color instantly when it lands in the hot pool.  At room temperature, the car retains its dark, light, or partially-shifted color (see my customer photos).  A feature that I really like, is that when you launch one car, it triggers the release of a second car if you have placed a second car under the spray pipe, so that the two cars will exit the stunt playset simultaneously (at the bottom on the ramps at the left and the right).  You can adjust how the launched car will exit--you can set a ramp in the middle to either crash the car into the hot-water pool or allow the car to jump the pool and continue down the track to the exit.  Because the two exit ramps can be connected to other tracks, you can incorporate this messy water stunt playset into a larger Hot Wheels layout!  The Color-Shifter cars apparently vary from playset to playset.  My playset came with a Nitro Doorslammer (dark green with cold water, light green with warm water).  Additional Color-Shifter cars (e.g., [[ASIN:B00IVP9UHA 24/SEVEN]]) are available from Amazon, but a regular Hot Wheels car that weighs about the same as the included car works fine.  The playset instructions caution that all Hot Wheels/Hot Wheels Color-Shifter cars may not work in every playset.  This playset's box has "City" printed on the front, so I would guess that all Color-Shifter cars in the "City" series will work with it.  Some reviewers have complained that the cars won't stay on the tracks, but I didn't have this problem.  The launcher has three tension settings, and my cars negotiated the tracks perfectly as long as I launched them with the first or second tension setting.  The track needs to be snapped together firmly for it to work.  The assembly instructions are easy to follow, although there are some tricky parts.

This UBTECH JIMU ROBOT-BUILDERBOTS KIT (Interactive Building Block System (357 Piece)) is so beautifully packaged, you’d almost think it was a smartphone or an Apple product.  It comes in a heavy-duty, magnetic-closure box that opens like a book.  Inside, the main controller, connectors and fasteners are packed in colorful paper boxes that nest perfectly in the big outer box.  Is such a lovely unboxing experience wasted on a kid’s toy?  This kit will make either a “Grabberbot” or a “Digbot”.  I say “either”, because the building components are extremely hard to take apart--you are NOT going to want to try a rebuild of either completed robot.  Some (but not all) of the components are similar to LEGO Technic elements, but they’re smaller and definitely NOT compatible with LEGO.  In an afternoon (about 4 hours), I built the Grabberbot, which I like a lot.  Using the Jimu app (iOS or Android), it’s easy to drive, very responsive, and actually picks things up.  Grabberbot has a cool, multicolored LED light (you can program custom colors), and he has a voice cube with a large repertoire of sounds (also programmable).  Grabberbot is controlled with Bluetooth--pairing is easy, once he’s turned on or plugged into a power source.  He does NOT have a separate controller, so you must have a tablet or a smartphone to make him work.  (I use an iPad Mini.)  It’s a good idea to charge the controller cube before you start building--it takes an hour or so.  You do NOT want to spend an afternoon building a robot, and then have to wait for the battery to charge before you can play with it!  You can’t build the Grabberbot without a tablet or smartphone--you have to download the app to get the building instructions.  (This is a pet peeve of mine; I believe that the instructions for building toys belong in the box.)  However, in defense of the download-only instructions, they are presented in 3D!  If you’re unsure about a particular step, you can rotate the model 360 degrees and look at it from every angle until you’re sure that you’ve got it right.  Grabberbot is more difficult to build than a motorized LEGO set.  There are three reasons for this: (1) the instructions don’t always show the elements recognizably or exactly the way they go together (e.g., the sensor box has fewer connector holes than the one shown in the instructions); (2) the instructions don’t always start at the easiest place for the next step in the assembly; and (3) the components click together so firmly, you need pliers to separate some of them if you make a mistake.  Sadly, the color-changing LED light needs to be programmed if you want it to do more than flash colored lights for a few seconds when Grabberbot is first turned on.  I did very little with the programming portion of the app, which requires you to identify which of the four servo motors you want to give instructions to (they are numbered), to choose commands for the motor(s), and to name and record the resulting action.  Programming doesn’t seem to be hard, but it does take some study and practice.  There is a charger with an AC adapter included.  The red light on the controller box changes to green when the battery is fully charged.  A firmware update starts automatically when you first connect the Grabberbot; it takes only a few minutes.

[[VIDEOID:98c98e0930a4d0d55067b255c77f0092]] UPDATE: It does not seem that you can load your own roms. Also, it seems the language selection is only for the menu, the ROMs are the same and that means titles that aren't English won't be no matter what. Super Mario Bros 3 for example, has the text bubbles from Toad at the end of each world in what appears to be Japanese. I'm guessing this file is a rip from the Japanese cartridge. I tried connecting the device to a computer to see if I can access to the storage and perhaps update it in case they are standard NES rom files (and maybe remove the homebrew roms that we aren't interested in to keep the list manageable). No luck. I found a video teardown on Youtube to see if maybe there is a hidden storage card inside or something. Nothing. It's an OK game player if you have some games available that you actually want to play, but for the money I know there are others that are better and actually let you update what games you want/don't want (and add others if you have access to them).  Original Review: I tried one of these handhelds once before and was very disappointed in the lineup. Like that one, this claims hundreds of classic games, but a lot of them you've never heard of. In fact, many of them appear to mostly be weird knock offs or duplicate / slightly modified versions of games you have heard of.  That being said, the classics I wanted ARE here! The original NES Mario trilogy is here and can still be played with mostly muscle memory from 30+ years ago! I even introduced some of my kids to my favorite, Super Mario Bros 3, and had all the nostalgia watching them play too. Unfortunately there's also Super Mario 4, 5, 6 and a bunch of other bizarre titles that seems like hacks of existing games with minor modifications. In the rom community, these are often referred to as Homebrew titles. It's a shame that there's so many more of those than real released games to choose from, clogging up the game selection. If you didn't know it wasn't an actual title release, you might be confused as to what is a real game in here. In fact, even the "real" games seem slightly off. For example, the original Super Mario Bros has music that plays noticeable too fast. Like the game is on speed. Weird. There are other games I used to own that are not on here, I'm actually wondering if I can modify the storage and put my own NES roms in and take out the weird hack ones, but I haven't been able to pry it away from my kids long enough to experiment with it. I guess that's a good thing, I have to admit I am enjoying this product much more than I expected to. Much better than the last handheld classic player I tried.
